# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Post-Disaster Phase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-Disaster Phase (KTU 2024 Definition):** The post-disaster phase is the period **immediately following the impact of a hazardous event** and lasts until normalcy is restored in the affected community. It encompasses all **rescue, relief, rehabilitation, and reconstruction activities** aimed at saving lives, alleviating suffering, restoring essential services, and rebuilding the livelihoods of the affected population.

According to the **National Disaster Management Act (NDMA), 2005** and the **KTU 2024 PECET524 syllabus**, the post-disaster phase is broadly subdivided into three overlapping sub-phases:

| Sub-Phase | Time Window | Primary Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **Relief Phase** | 0 to 72 hours (Golden Period) | Life-saving, evacuation, emergency medical aid |
| **Rehabilitation Phase** | 72 hours to 6 months | Restoration of basic services, temporary shelter |
| **Recovery / Reconstruction Phase** | 6 months to several years | Permanent rebuilding, livelihood restoration |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Recovery Room"**
> Imagine a person who has just survived a major heart attack. The *post-disaster phase* is exactly like the **ICU → Recovery Ward → Home Discharge** timeline in a hospital:
> - **First 72 hours (ICU):** Stabilize the patient, ensure breathing and circulation (rescue & first aid).
> - **Next few weeks (Recovery Ward):** Provide medicines, nutrition, monitored rest (relief camps, food, water).
> - **Months later (Discharge & Rehab):** Physiotherapy, lifestyle change, return to normal life (reconstruction, livelihood).
>
> The patient's family, doctors, nurses, and social workers each play distinct, coordinated roles — just like **NDRF, Army, SDRF, NGOs, and local administration** in a real disaster.

## 1.3 Standard Operational Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sphere Standards (Key International Benchmarks adopted by NDMA):**
> - **Water:** Minimum **15 liters per person per day** (L/p/d) for all uses; **7.5 L/p/d** as absolute survival minimum.
> - **Food:** Minimum **2,100 Kcal/person/day** (energy) and **50 g/person/day** protein.
> - **Shelter:** Minimum **3.5 m² floor area per person** in emergency shelters (45 m² for 12-person family tent).
> - **Sanitation:** **1 toilet per 20 persons** (max) in emergency phase.
> - **Response Time:** **Golden 72 hours** for life-saving search & rescue.

## 1.4 Why the Post-Disaster Phase Matters

- **Mortality Reduction:** Approximately **90% of disaster deaths** can be prevented if relief reaches within the **Golden 72 hours**.
- **Secondary Disaster Prevention:** Lack of sanitation causes **cholera, dysentery, epidemics** that kill more people than the original event.
- **Economic Loss Mitigation:** Faster recovery reduces long-term GDP loss (e.g., **2001 Bhuj earthquake: ₹1,00,000 crore** loss).
- **Social Cohesion:** Equitable relief prevents unrest, displacement conflicts, and migration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework of Post-Disaster Relief

The post-disaster phase follows a **systematic 7-stage operational framework** as recognized by NDMA's National Disaster Response Plan (NDRP):

1. **Damage & Needs Assessment (DNA)**
   - Rapid assessment within first 24 hours using standardized formats.
   - Conducted by **District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA)** with support from NDRF teams.
   - Output: Preliminary damage report (PDNA — Preliminary Damage & Needs Assessment).

2. **Search, Rescue & Evacuation**
   - NDRF, SDRF, Army, Navy, Air Force, Civil Defence deployed.
   - Tools: **USAR — Urban Search and Rescue** teams, sniffer dogs, thermal imaging cameras, life detectors.
   - **Golden Window = 72 hours** (survivor survival curve drops steeply after this).

3. **Emergency Medical Response (EMR)**
   - Triage system: **Black (deceased), Red (immediate), Yellow (delayed), Green (walking wounded)**.
   - Establishment of **field hospitals, mobile medical units, and casualty clearing stations**.

4. **Relief Distribution — Life-Saving Supplies**
   - Food packets, drinking water, ORS, medicines, blankets, tarpaulins.
   - Managed through **Incident Response System (IRS)** and Emergency Operation Centres (EOC).

5. **Shelter & Camp Management**
   - **Relief camps** with standard amenities (UNHCR/IASC Camp Management standards).
   - Site selection: Higher ground, away from flood/water, accessible by road.

6. **Restoration of Essential Services**
   - Power, water supply, communication, transport, banking, schools.
   - **Critical Infrastructure Prioritization Matrix (CIPM)** is used.

7. **Rehabilitation, Recovery & Reconstruction**
   - **PDNA (Post-Disaster Needs Assessment)** conducted with World Bank, ADB support.
   - **Build Back Better (BBB)** principle as per Sendai Framework 2015.

## 2.2 Core Amenities Provided During Post-Disaster Phase

> [!NOTE]
> **The "5 Pillars of Relief Amenities" (KTU 2024 Exam-High-Yield Concept):**

### Pillar 1: Water, Sanitation & Hygiene (WASH)
- **Water supply:** Tanker trucks, bore wells with hand pumps, water purification units (e.g., **Nerus® filters**).
- **Sanitation:** Community latrines, solid waste disposal, vector control.
- **Hygiene kits:** Soap, sanitary napkins, ORS packets, chlorine tablets.

### Pillar 2: Food & Nutrition
- **Dry rations** (rice, wheat, pulses, oil, salt, sugar) for 15 days per family.
- **Hot cooked meals** through community kitchens (langar model).
- **Special nutrition** for children (<5 yrs), pregnant & lactating mothers (Take-Home Rations).

### Pillar 3: Shelter & Non-Food Items (NFI)
- Tarpaulins, tents, blankets, kitchen sets, hurricane lanterns, clothing.
- **Disaster-resistant temporary shelters** (ISO containers, prefab units).

### Pillar 4: Health & Medical Care
- First aid, trauma care, immunization (especially **measles vaccination in camps**).
- Mental Health & Psychosocial Support (MHPSS).

### Pillar 5: Protection & Education
- Child protection, women safety, elderly care.
- **Temporary Learning Centres (TLCs)** within 2 weeks of disaster.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations, Ratios & Standards for KTU 2024 Exam**

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit / Value | Source / Authority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water requirement per person per day | $W = 15 \text{ L/p/d}$ (normal) | Litres | Sphere Standard |
| Survival minimum water | $W_{min} = 7.5 \text{ L/p/d}$ | Litres | WHO |
| Food energy per person per day | $E = 2{,}100 \text{ Kcal/p/d}$ | Kilocalories | Sphere |
| Protein requirement | $P = 50 \text{ g/p/d}$ | Grams | WHO |
| Floor area per person in shelter | $A = 3.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{p}$ | m² | UNHCR |
| Toilet ratio | $R_t = 1:20$ (max) | Ratio | Sphere |
| Total relief cost | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times U_i) + L + T$ | ₹/USD | Standard |
| Golden period | $t_g = 72 \text{ hours}$ | Hours | NDMA |
| Population coverage time | $T_c = \dfrac{N_{total} \times R_p}{D_c \times n_t}$ | Days | Logistics formula |
| Triage categories | 4 (Black, Red, Yellow, Green) | Count | WHO START |

> Where:
> - $Q_i$ = quantity of relief item $i$, $U_i$ = unit cost, $L$ = logistics cost, $T$ = transport cost
> - $N_{total}$ = total affected population, $R_p$ = per-person ration, $D_c$ = daily distribution capacity, $n_t$ = number of distribution trucks

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Field Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Designing **disaster-resistant temporary shelters** with bamboo, ferrocement, ISO containers.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Reverse osmosis water purifiers, mobile generators, cold chain vehicles.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Solar PV-powered relief camps, satellite-based communication (VSAT, INMARSAT).
- **Computer/IT:** GIS-based damage mapping, **Disaster Management Information System (DMIS)**, drone-based aerial assessment.
- **Public Health:** Epidemic surveillance, water quality testing (MFT — Most Probable Number for coliforms).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Relief Camp Capacity Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A flood has affected a village of **4,800 people**. The district administration plans to set up **relief camps** using **12-person family tents**, each with floor area of **45 m²**. Each camp site can host a maximum of **20 tents**. Calculate:
> 1. Total number of tents required.
> 2. Total floor area required.
> 3. Number of water tankers needed (each tanker = 5,000 L, supply once daily).
> 4. Number of toilet seats required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Total tents required**

$$
N_{tents} = \frac{N_{people}}{C_{tent}}
$$

$$
N_{tents} = \frac{4{,}800}{12} = 400 \text{ tents}
$$

**Step 2: Number of camp sites required**

$$
N_{sites} = \frac{N_{tents}}{T_{per\_site}} = \frac{400}{20} = 20 \text{ camp sites}
$$

**Step 3: Total floor area required**

$$
A_{total} = N_{tents} \times A_{tent} = 400 \times 45 = 18{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 1.8 \text{ hectares}
$$

**Step 4: Total daily water requirement (using Sphere Standard 15 L/p/d)**

$$
W_{daily} = N_{people} \times W_{per} = 4{,}800 \times 15 = 72{,}000 \text{ L/day}
$$

**Step 5: Number of water tankers required (round-up)**

$$
N_{tanker} = \left\lceil \frac{W_{daily}}{C_{tanker}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{72{,}000}{5{,}000} \right\rceil = \lceil 14.4 \rceil = 15 \text{ tankers}
$$

**Step 6: Number of toilet seats (Sphere 1:20 ratio)**

$$
N_{toilets} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{people}}{20} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{4{,}800}{20} \right\rceil = 240 \text{ seats}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Notice how the **5,000 L tanker capacity** is the limiting factor. In real operations, engineers add a **15% safety margin** for spillage, delays, and breakdowns — pushing actual tanker count to **18 tankers** for 4,800 people.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Relief Cost Estimation

**Problem Statement:**
> Estimate the total relief cost for 4,800 flood-affected people for a 15-day period, given:
> - Dry ration per family (5 members): ₹3,500 for 15 days
> - Medical cost per person: ₹250
> - Shelter cost per person: ₹800
> - Logistics overhead: 12% of direct cost
> - Transport: ₹50,000 per day

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Number of families**

$$
N_{fam} = \frac{N_{people}}{5} = \frac{4{,}800}{5} = 960 \text{ families}
$$

**Step 2: Dry ration cost (15 days)**

$$
C_{ration} = 960 \times 3{,}500 = \text{₹}33{,}60{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Medical cost**

$$
C_{med} = 4{,}800 \times 250 = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Shelter cost**

$$
C_{shelter} = 4{,}800 \times 800 = \text{₹}38{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Direct cost total**

$$
C_{direct} = C_{ration} + C_{med} + C_{shelter}
$$

$$
C_{direct} = 33{,}60{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 38{,}40{,}000 = \text{₹}84{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Logistics overhead (12%)**

$$
C_{log} = 0.12 \times 84{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}10{,}08{,}000
$$

**Step 7: Transport cost (15 days)**

$$
C_{trans} = 50{,}000 \times 15 = \text{₹}7{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 8: Total relief cost**

$$
C_{total} = C_{direct} + C_{log} + C_{trans}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 84{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}08{,}000 + 7{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}01{,}58{,}000
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer: ₹1.0158 Crore ≈ ₹1.02 Crore** for 4,800 people × 15 days.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Damage Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Classification Table (Frequently tested as 3-mark question):**

| Damage Category | Buildings | Population Displaced | Response Level | Lead Agency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **L0 / L1 — Minor** | Few houses damaged | < 500 | District level | DDMA |
| **L2 — Moderate** | Village/town affected | 500 – 5,000 | State level | SDMA |
| **L3 — Severe** | District affected | 5,000 – 50,000 | National level | NDMA + MHA |
| **L4 / L5 — Catastrophic** | Multi-district / State | > 50,000 | International | UN agencies |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define the post-disaster phase. List any two main activities carried out in this phase.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
> The post-disaster phase is the period immediately after the occurrence of a disaster, lasting until normalcy is restored in the affected community. It includes **rescue, relief, rehabilitation, and reconstruction** activities.
>
> **Two main activities:**
> 1. **Search, Rescue and Evacuation** of trapped victims (NDRF, SDRF, Army).
> 2. **Provision of relief amenities** — food, water, shelter, medical aid, sanitation (as per Sphere Standards).

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: State the Sphere Standards for water supply and floor area in emergency shelters.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
> - **Water supply:** Minimum **15 litres per person per day** (L/p/d) for all uses; absolute survival minimum is **7.5 L/p/d**.
> - **Floor area in emergency shelter:** Minimum **3.5 m² per person** (45 m² for a standard 12-person family tent).
>
> These standards are adopted by NDMA and the National Disaster Response Plan (NDRP).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**Q: Explain in detail the operational framework of post-disaster relief management in India. Discuss the role of NDRF, SDMA, and NGOs with reference to the National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP), 2019.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Operational Framework of Post-Disaster Relief Management in India (7 Marks)**

The Government of India follows a **multi-tiered operational framework** under the **National Disaster Management Act, 2005** and the **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP), 2019**. The framework operates at four levels — **National, State, District, and Community**.

1. **Activation of Incident Response System (IRS):** Once a disaster strikes, the District Magistrate (DM) activates the IRS and the Emergency Operation Centre (EOC). The IRS has a standardized structure — Incident Commander, Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance Sections.

2. **Damage and Needs Assessment (DNA):** Within the first 24 hours, teams conduct rapid assessments using the **Preliminary Damage & Needs Assessment (PDNA)** format. This helps quantify human, livestock, infrastructure, and livelihood losses.

3. **Search, Rescue & Evacuation:** **NDRF battalions (16 battalions of 1,149 personnel each)** are deployed along with SDRF, Army, Navy, Air Force, and Civil Defence. The **Golden 72-hour window** is critical.

4. **Emergency Medical Response:** Triage-based medical care with field hospitals, helicopter evacuations, and epidemic surveillance (especially for diarrhoea, cholera, measles).

5. **Relief Distribution:** Food packets, drinking water, ORS, blankets, tarpaulins, and cash transfers (DBT) through DSO (District Supply Office) under the supervision of SDO and Tehsildar.

6. **Camp Management:** Relief camps follow **UNHCR/IASC standards** — minimum 3.5 m²/person floor area, 1 toilet per 20 persons, 15 L water/p/d, 2,100 Kcal food/p/d.

7. **Restoration & Reconstruction:** Conducted under the **Build Back Better (BBB)** principle of the **Sendai Framework 2015**.

**(b) Role of NDRF, SDMA, and NGOs (7 Marks)**

**1. National Disaster Response Force (NDRF):**
- Apex specialized force for disaster response, raised in **2006** under **Section 44 of DM Act 2005**.
- **16 battalions** drawn from BSF, CRPF, CISF, ITBP, SSB, etc.
- Trained in **USAR, CBRN, flood rescue, heli-cast, and deep diving**.
- Self-contained with engineering, medical, communication, and canine squads.

**2. State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA):**
- Chaired by the **Chief Minister** of the state.
- Approves state disaster plans, coordinates with NDMA, ensures funds, equipment, and training.
- Activates **State Disaster Response Force (SDRF)** and **State EOC**.
- Conducts mock drills, capacity building, and relief fund disbursement (SDRF — State Disaster Response Fund under SDRF guidelines of GoI).

**3. Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs):**
- Examples: **Indian Red Cross Society, SEEDS, Goonj, Helpage India, Oxfam India, Sphere India**.
- Roles: **Last-mile delivery**, community mobilization, psychosocial support, gender-sensitive relief, WASH, shelter construction.
- Work under the **umbrella of NITI Aayog's NGO-Darpan portal** and coordinate through **Sphere India** (the national humanitarian platform).
- Crucial for **vulnerable groups** — women, children, elderly, PwD, SC/ST communities.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **write only the names** of agencies and lose marks. To score full marks, you **must describe their specific functions, legal basis, and inter-linkages** (e.g., mention Section 44 of DM Act 2005 for NDRF, mention Sendai Framework for BBB principle).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**Q: What are the essential amenities to be provided in a post-disaster relief camp? Describe the "5 Pillars of Relief Amenities" with reference to Sphere Standards and explain how a Post-Disaster Needs Assessment (PDNA) is conducted.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**Model Answer:**

**(a) 5 Pillars of Relief Amenities — Sphere Standards (7 Marks)**

The **Sphere Standards (2018 edition)**, adopted globally and by NDMA India, define minimum standards in **5 core areas** of humanitarian response. These are mandatory benchmarks in relief camps.

**Pillar 1: Water, Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH)**
- **Water:** 15 L/person/day (3 L drinking + 2 L cooking + 10 L hygiene).
- **Sanitation:** 1 toilet per 20 persons; separate toilets for women & men; defecation fields if latrines not feasible.
- **Hygiene:** Soap 250 g/person/month, sanitary napkins, baby diapers, ORS, water purification tablets (chlorine).

**Pillar 2: Food Security and Nutrition**
- **Energy:** 2,100 Kcal/person/day.
- **Protein:** 50 g/person/day; Fat: 30–35% of energy.
- **Micronutrients:** Vitamin A, Iron, Iodine fortified rations.
- Special rations for **children <5 yrs** (CSB++ — Corn Soya Blend) and **pregnant/lactating women** (THR — Take Home Ration).

**Pillar 3: Shelter, Housing and Non-Food Items (NFI)**
- **3.5 m²/person** covered living area in emergency shelters.
- Tarpaulins, blankets (1 per family member), sleeping mats, kitchen sets, lanterns, mosquito nets.
- Cultural appropriateness and climate-resilience of materials.

**Pillar 4: Health Action**
- Primary healthcare within camp, immunization (measles + Vitamin A for children 6 mo–15 yrs).
- Referral to higher centres, ambulance services, MHPSS (Mental Health & Psychosocial Support).
- Disease surveillance (IDSP — Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme).

**Pillar 5: Protection and Education**
- Child Protection (against trafficking, abuse), GBV (Gender-Based Violence) prevention, women safety.
- **Temporary Learning Centres (TLCs)** within 2 weeks; Education in Emergencies kits.

**(b) Post-Disaster Needs Assessment (PDNA) — Methodology (7 Marks)**

The **PDNA** is a government-led process (with **World Bank, ADB, UN**) to assess damage, loss, and recovery needs. Conducted typically **2 weeks to 3 months** after the disaster.

**PDNA Phases:**

1. **Phase 1: Pre-Assessment Coordination** (Week 1)
   - Government announces PDNA; sets up PDNA Secretariat.
   - Sectoral Working Groups (SWGs) formed: Social, Infrastructure, Economic, Cross-cutting.

2. **Phase 2: Data Collection & Field Assessment** (Weeks 2–6)
   - Household surveys, satellite imagery (ISRO CARTOSAT), drone surveys, GIS mapping.
   - Damage = Replacement cost of destroyed assets.
   - Loss = Foregone economic flow (income, production).

3. **Phase 3: Sectoral Assessment** (Weeks 4–8)
   - Housing, Health, Education, Transport, Power, Water, Agriculture, Livelihoods, Tourism, Cultural Heritage.
   - Each SWG submits sectoral damage & loss (D&L) report.

4. **Phase 4: Recovery Strategy & Needs Estimation** (Weeks 6–10)
   - Recovery needs = Total D&L + Build Back Better premium (~15–30%).
   - Recovery strategy: Short-term (6 months), Medium-term (1–3 years), Long-term (3–10 years).

5. **Phase 5: Validation & Donor Conference**
   - Validation workshop, government approval, **Donor Conference** (e.g., 2015 Nepal earthquake, 2013 Uttarakhand floods, 2018 Kerala floods).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse **PDNA (post-disaster)** with **DNA (Damage & Needs Assessment — pre-disaster rapid assessment)**. They are different in scope, methodology, and timeline. Also, students often miss mentioning the **Build Back Better premium** and lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PECET524 Module 4**

### Core Definitions
- **Post-Disaster Phase:** Period from disaster impact until normalcy.
- **Golden Period:** 72 hours for life-saving.
- **PDNA:** Post-Disaster Needs Assessment (2 weeks – 3 months).
- **Sphere Standards:** International humanitarian benchmarks (4th edition 2018).

### Numerical Standards (Memorize)
- Water: **15 L/p/d** (min 7.5 L/p/d)
- Food: **2,100 Kcal/p/d**, Protein **50 g/p/d**
- Shelter: **3.5 m²/p**
- Toilets: **1:20 ratio**
- Golden window: **72 hours**
- Flood rescue: **NDRF**; **16 battalions** in India

### Key Legal/Policy Documents
- **DM Act 2005** (Section 14 — NDMA, Section 25 — SDMA, Section 44 — NDRF)
- **NDMP 2019** (National Disaster Management Plan)
- **Sendai Framework 2015** (4 priorities for action, BBB principle)
- **SDRF & SDRF guidelines** (Ministry of Home Affairs)

### Important Organizations
- **NDRF, SDRF, DDMA, SDMA, NDMA**
- **Indian Red Cross, NITI Aayog, Sphere India**
- **IMD, INCOIS, ISRO, CWC** (early warning agencies)

### Sub-Phases of Post-Disaster
1. **Relief** (0–72 hrs) → 2. **Rehabilitation** (72 hrs – 6 mo) → 3. **Reconstruction** (6 mo – years)

### 5 Pillars of Relief Amenities
**WASH, Food, Shelter/NFI, Health, Protection/Education** — all aligned with Sphere Standards.

### High-Yield Terms
- Triage: **Black, Red, Yellow, Green** (START protocol)
- IRS: **Incident Response System**
- BBB: **Build Back Better**
- DBT: **Direct Benefit Transfer** (cash relief)
- MHPSS: **Mental Health & Psychosocial Support**
- EOC: **Emergency Operation Centre**

### Common Exam Tricks
- Always quote **specific numbers, sections, dates, and Acts** — examiners reward precision.
- For numericals, add a **10–15% safety margin** (real-world practice).
- Distinguish **DNA (rapid)** vs **PDNA (detailed)** — frequent confusion.
- Mention **"Sendai Framework 2015"** wherever reconstruction is discussed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET524 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: RELIEF CAMPS – SECTION 1
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relief Camp (KTU/NDMA Terminology):** A *Relief Camp* is a **temporarily organized, planned, and managed settlement** established in a safe location to provide **collective life-saving assistance** — shelter, food, water, medical care, sanitation, and psychosocial support — to **disaster-displaced populations (DDPs)** who have lost their habitual place of residence or are unable to return to it for an extended duration.

According to the **Sphere Handbook (2018 Edition)** and the **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP) of India (2019)**, a relief camp must be operational within the **first 24–72 hours (Golden Phase)** of displacement and must adhere to minimum humanitarian standards.

**Key Synonyms Used in KTU Curriculum:**
- Emergency Shelter Camp
- Temporary Settlement / Transit Camp
- Emergency Accommodation Centre
- Collective Centre (IASC terminology)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The School Auditorium During a Flood"**
>
> Imagine your college is suddenly turned into a hostel overnight. Walls are divided into rooms (zones), a water tap is fixed in the corridor, a temporary kitchen is set up in the canteen, the sickroom becomes a medical post, and the ground becomes a helipad. The Principal (Camp Manager) is now in charge not of education, but of **300 strangers' lives, food, safety, and dignity**.
>
> That *organized overnight transformation* is exactly what a **Relief Camp** is — a **mini-city built in hours**, not years, by disaster managers.

### 1.3 Why Relief Camps Are a Critical Topic in KTU 2024

- Direct mapping to **SDG 11** (Sustainable Cities & Communities) and **SDG 13** (Climate Action).
- Aligned with **Sendai Framework Priority 4** — "Enhancing disaster preparedness for effective response."
- Forms high-yield 14-mark questions due to its operational, multi-disciplinary nature (Civil + Public Health + Management).

### 1.4 Classification at a Glance

| Parameter | Type 1 | Type 2 | Type 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Duration | < 72 hours | 1 week – 3 months | > 3 months |
| Population | 50–200 | 200–1,000 | 1,000–50,000+ |
| Infrastructure | Tents / Open ground | Pre-existing buildings | Planned tented township |
| KTU Example | Flood evacuation | Cyclone shelter | Bhuj Earthquake 2001 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 4: RELIEF CAMPS – SECTION 2
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Camp Typology (Based on Origin of Displacement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Classification (Per KTU Module 4 Syllabus):**
>
> 1. **Spontaneous / Self-Evolving Camps** – Created *by* the affected people themselves, *without* external coordination (e.g., open-ground congregations after the 2013 Uttarakhand floods).
> 2. **Planned / Assisted Camps** – Established *by* the government, military, or NGOs with full site planning, zoning, and service delivery (e.g., Panchayat-ground relief camps in Kerala during 2018 floods).
> 3. **Transit / Evacuation Camps** – Short-duration holding areas used during active evacuation (e.g., railway-station holding areas during a tsunami alert).
> 4. **Transitional Shelters** – Semi-permanent structures (bunkhouses, prefabricated units) used as a bridge between tents and permanent reconstruction (used by UN-Habitat in the Nepal 2015 response).
> 5. **Reception / Registration Centres** – First-point-of-contact facilities for registration, family tracing, and onward referral.

### 2.2 Site Selection Criteria (High-Yield for 14-Mark Questions)

The site selection process follows a **4-Filter Hierarchical Screening Model**:

1. **Safety Filter**
   - Outside flood-plain, landslide zone, and coastal high-hazard zone.
   - Minimum **3 meters above the High Flood Level (HFL)** for riverine floods.
   - Minimum **500 meters from hazardous industries**, LPG plants, and electrical substations.

2. **Accessibility Filter**
   - Approachable by **all-weather road** for trucks carrying relief material.
   - Helipad-clear area of minimum **30 m × 30 m** (Day) / **50 m × 50 m** (Night-lit).
   - Proximity to **functional hospital** within 10 km radius.

3. **Capacity Filter**
   - Minimum land area: **30–45 sq. meters per person** (Sphere Standard for planned camps).
   - Total site area = Population × **45 m²/person**.

4. **Livelihoods & Dignity Filter**
   - Distance from original village ≤ 5 km (to enable livelihood recovery).
   - Availability of **defensible space**, gender-separated WASH facilities, and cultural/religious accommodation.

### 2.3 Layout & Zoning of a Planned Relief Camp

A relief camp is divided into **6 functional zones**:

| Zone No. | Zone Name | Function | % of Total Area |
|---|---|---|---|
| Z-1 | Accommodation Zone | Family tents / shelter units | 40% |
| Z-2 | WASH Zone | Water, toilets, bathing, laundry | 10% |
| Z-3 | Food & Kitchen Zone | Central kitchen, storage, child feeding | 10% |
| Z-4 | Health Zone | OPD, IPD, isolation, mental-health PSS | 5% |
| Z-5 | Service Zone | Admin, registration, police post, child-friendly space | 10% |
| Z-6 | Logistics Zone | Warehouses, parking, helipad, generator area | 25% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"WAFHSL"** → **W**ASH, **A**ccommodation, **F**ood, **H**ealth, **S**ervice, **L**ogistics.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — Sphere Standards for Camps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sphere Minimum Standards (2018 Edition) — Memorize the Numerics**

| Indicator | Minimum Standard | Unit | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Covered living space per person | **3.5** | m² | Sphere – Shelter Standard 1 |
| Total camp area per person | **45** | m² | Sphere – Shelter Standard 2 |
| Water supply per person/day | **15** | litres | Sphere – WASH Standard 2.1 |
| Toilet : Person ratio | **1 : 20** (max) | — | Sphere – WASH Standard 3.2 |
| Bathing : Person ratio | **1 : 50** (max) | — | Sphere – WASH Standard 3.3 |
| Calorie intake per person/day | **2,100** | kcal | Sphere – Food Standard 1.1 |
| Distance to nearest toilet | **≤ 50** | m | Sphere – WASH Standard 3.4 |
| Distance to nearest water point | **≤ 200** | m | Sphere – WASH Standard 2.2 |
| Primary health care staff | **1 : 1,000** (max) | ratio | Sphere – Health Standard 1.1 |
| Cold-chain storage | **+2 to +8** | °C | WHO/EPI Standard |

### 2.5 Standard Camp Management Structure (Nodal Hierarchy)

> **District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA) — Apex**
>          ↓
> **Sub-Divisional Magistrate (SDM) / Sub-Collector — Overall In-Charge**
>          ↓
> **Camp-In-Charge (Officer-level, IAS/PCS cadre)**
>          ↓
> **6 Cell-Heads** (Shelter / WASH / Food / Health / Protection / Logistics)

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Planning Utility

- **Civil Engineering** – Design of elevated platforms, drainage layout, temporary structure loads.
- **Environmental Engineering** – Grey-water soak-pit design (e.g., $V_{\text{soak}} = 1.5 \times Q_{\text{peak}}$).
- **Public Health** – Disease surveillance (Cholera, Leptospirosis, Measles, ARI).
- **Management** – Supply-chain logistics, HR planning, finance & accountability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 4: RELIEF CAMPS – SECTION 3
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Computations & Camp-Sizing Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Provide the student with **calculation-ready, KTU-exam-style** numerical and analytical frameworks for relief-camp planning.

### 3.1 Numerical 1 — Sizing a Camp for a Given Population

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Numerical):**
> A district administration must establish a relief camp for **4,000 displaced flood victims**. Compute the required (a) total land area, (b) number of toilets, (c) daily water requirement, and (d) number of primary health workers.

**Given Data (from KTU Sphere Table):**
- Land area per person = $45 \, \text{m}^2/\text{person}$
- Toilet : Person ratio = $1 : 20$
- Water per person per day = $15 \, \text{L}$
- Health staff ratio = $1 : 1{,}000$

**Step 1 — Total Land Area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{total}} &= N \times a_{\text{per\,person}} \\
                 &= 4{,}000 \times 45 \\
                 &= 180{,}000 \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion to more intuitive units:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{total}} &= \frac{180{,}000}{10{,}000} \, \text{hectares} \\
                 &= 18 \, \text{hectares} \approx 44.5 \, \text{acres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Toilets**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{required}} &= \frac{N}{\text{Ratio}} \\
                   &= \frac{4{,}000}{20} \\
                   &= 200 \, \text{toilets}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subdivision (Gender-Sensitive Planning):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{female}} &= 0.50 \times 200 = 100 \\
T_{\text{male}}   &= 0.40 \times 200 = 80 \\
T_{\text{child}}  &= 0.10 \times 200 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily Water Requirement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{water}} &= N \times q_{\text{per\,capita}} \\
                &= 4{,}000 \times 15 \\
                &= 60{,}000 \, \text{L/day} = 60 \, \text{kL/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of taps required** (assuming 1 tap serves 250 persons per Sphere Standard 2.2.3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Taps} &= \frac{4{,}000}{250} = 16 \, \text{taps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Health Workforce**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{staff}} &= \frac{4{,}000}{1{,}000} = 4 \, \text{doctors/paramedics}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Numerical 2 — Food & Calorie Planning

**Problem:** Provide daily ration planning for 4,000 people at **2,100 kcal/person/day**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{daily}} &= 4{,}000 \times 2{,}100 = 8.4 \times 10^6 \, \text{kcal/day} \\
E_{\text{weekly}} &= 8.4 \times 10^6 \times 7 = 5.88 \times 10^7 \, \text{kcal/week}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Standard WHO/ICMR Daily Ration Composition per Person:**

| Item | Quantity (g) | kcal Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| Rice / Wheat | 400 g | 1,440 |
| Dal (Pulses) | 80 g | 280 |
| Oil | 25 g | 225 |
| Salt | 10 g | 0 |
| Vegetables | 200 g | 80 |
| Sugar / Jaggery | 30 g | 120 |
| **Total** | **745 g** | **≈ 2,145 kcal** ✓ |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Camp-Decision Flowchart Logic

The following **decision hierarchy** is what KTU examiners test in **14-mark descriptive questions**:

1. **Has the State declared an emergency?** → If No → State-level monitoring only.
2. **Is the displaced population > 500?** → If Yes → Open a *Planned Camp* (not spontaneous).
3. **Is land available within 5 km of the original habitat?** → If No → Open a *Transitional Shelter* programme.
4. **Is the camp duration projected > 3 months?** → If Yes → Switch to *T-Shelter* (Transitional Shelter) + *WASH* upgrade.
5. **Is disease outbreak detected (e.g., cholera)?** → Activate *Isolation Ward* and *WASH Emergency Response Team* within 24 hours.

### 3.4 Capacity-Buffering Calculation (Logistics)

**Standard Buffer Formula (Used by UNHCR & NDMA):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Buffer Stock} &= 1.20 \times \text{Initial Requirement} \\
\text{Reason}        &= 20\% \text{ reserve for pipeline failure, in-transit loss, surge demand}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Applied to our 4,000-person camp:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{buffer}} &= 1.20 \times 60{,}000 = 72{,}000 \, \text{L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Risk-Index Calculation for a Camp Site

A **Camp Site Risk Index (CSRI)** can be computed as a weighted sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CSRI} &= 0.30 F + 0.25 H + 0.20 A + 0.15 E + 0.10 S
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $F$ = Flood-risk score (0–10)
- $H$ = Health-hazard proximity score (0–10)
- $A$ = Accessibility score (0–10)
- $E$ = Environmental sensitivity score (0–10)
- $S$ = Social-cohesion / community-acceptance score (0–10)

**Interpretation:**

| CSRI Range | Classification | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 3 | Low Risk | Use as planned camp |
| 4 – 6 | Moderate Risk | Use with mitigation measures |
| 7 – 10 | High Risk | Reject the site |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 4: RELIEF CAMPS – SECTION 4
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Functional Architecture of a Relief Camp

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DDMA Apex Authority] --> B[Sub Collector SDM Nodal]
    B --> C[Camp In Charge Officer]
    C --> D1[Shelter Cell]
    C --> D2[WASH Cell]
    C --> D3[Food and Nutrition Cell]
    C --> D4[Health Cell]
    C --> D5[Protection Cell]
    C --> D6[Logistics Cell]
    D1 --> E1[Tent Allocation]
    D1 --> E2[Family Tracing]
    D2 --> F1[Water Points]
    D2 --> F2[Toilet Blocks]
    D2 --> F3[Bathing Units]
    D3 --> G1[Central Kitchen]
    D3 --> G2[Child Feeding Centre]
    D3 --> G3[Food Warehouse]
    D4 --> H1[OPD and IPD]
    D4 --> H2[Isolation Ward]
    D4 --> H3[Psychosocial PSS Unit]
    D5 --> I1[Women Friendly Space]
    D5 --> I2[Child Friendly Space]
    D5 --> I3[Elderly Corner]
    D6 --> J1[Warehouse]
    D6 --> J2[Helipad]
    D6 --> J3[Generator and Fuel Yard]
    D6 --> J4[Transport Pool]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Camp Establishment (Hour-by-Hour Timeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H0[Hour 0 to 6: Recon and Site Selection] --> H1[Hour 6 to 12: Tents and WASH Setup]
    H1 --> H2[Hour 12 to 24: Registration and Food]
    H2 --> H3[Hour 24 to 48: Health and Protection Cells]
    H3 --> H4[Hour 48 to 72: Stabilization and Reporting]
    H4 --> H5[Day 4 to 7: WASH Upgrade and Child Friendly Space]
    H5 --> H6[Week 2 Onwards: Recovery and Transition Planning]
    H6 --> H7[Final Phase: Camp Closure and Repatriation]
```

### 4.3 Camp Layout — Block-Level Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[LOGISTICS ZONE 25%]
        LP[Helipad 30m x 30m] --> WH[Warehouse Cluster]
        WH --> GY[Generator Yard]
        GY --> TP[Transport Parking]
    end
    subgraph S1[SERVICE ZONE 10%]
        AD[Admin Office] --> PO[Police Aid Post]
        PO --> CF[Child Friendly Space]
        CF --> WF[Women Friendly Space]
    end
    subgraph A1[ACCOMMODATION ZONE 40%]
        F1[Family Block A 1000 persons] --> F2[Family Block B 1000 persons]
        F2 --> F3[Family Block C 1000 persons]
        F3 --> F4[Family Block D 1000 persons]
    end
    subgraph W1[WASH ZONE 10%]
        WP[Water Point Network] --> TB[Toilet Blocks 200 units]
        TB --> BU[Bathing Units]
        BU --> LD[Laundry and Drying Yard]
    end
    subgraph F1K[FOOD ZONE 10%]
        CK[Central Kitchen] --> CW[Clean Water Store]
        CW --> FW[Food Warehouse]
        FW --> CFC[Child Feeding Centre]
    end
    subgraph H1K[HEALTH ZONE 5%]
        OPD[OPD and IPD] --> ISO[Isolation Ward 10 beds]
        ISO --> PSS[Psychosocial Support]
    end
    L1 --- S1
    S1 --- A1
    A1 --- W1
    W1 --- F1K
    F1K --- H1K
```

### 4.4 Stakeholder Coordination Architecture (Information Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V[Affected Victims and Families] --> R[Camp In Charge]
    R --> N[NGO Partners Example Red Cross and Oxfam]
    R --> G[Government Line Departments]
    R --> M[Military and NDRF SDRF]
    R --> U[UN Agencies Example UNHCR and UNICEF]
    N --> R
    G --> R
    M --> R
    U --> R
    R --> ME[Media and Communication Cell]
    ME --> PUB[Public Information Bulletins]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual-Learning Tip:** Always draw the **6-Zone model** in your answer sheet using a simple rectangular layout with percentages. Examiners award easy marks for **labelled diagrams**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 4: RELIEF CAMPS – SECTION 5
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **Define a relief camp. List any four Sphere standards that govern the operation of a relief camp. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A *relief camp* is a temporary planned settlement providing life-saving assistance (shelter, food, water, medical, sanitation) to disaster-displaced persons who cannot return home.

**Four Sphere Standards (any four):**
1. Minimum **45 m² total land area** per person.
2. Minimum **3.5 m² covered living area** per person.
3. Minimum **15 L of water** per person per day.
4. Toilet to person ratio of **1 : 20 maximum**.
5. Distance to nearest water point ≤ **200 m**.

**Mark Split (as per KTU Valuation Key):** Definition 1 M + 4 Standards (0.5 M each) = 1 + 2 = 3 M.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Differentiate between a Spontaneous Camp and a Planned Camp. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Spontaneous Camp | Planned Camp |
|---|---|---|
| Initiator | Affected community itself | Government / NGO / Military |
| Site Planning | None / haphazard | Engineered layout with zoning |
| Service Delivery | Irregular | Standardized (Sphere-compliant) |
| Sustainability | Short, chaotic | Medium to long, structured |
| Example | Open-ground congregation (Uttarakhand 2013) | Kerala 2018 flood relief camps |

---

### 📘 PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Model Paper 1]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **criteria for site selection of a relief camp**, with reference to safety, accessibility, capacity, and dignity. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **standard layout of a relief camp** with the percentage allocation of land for each functional zone. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — PART (a)

**1. Introduction (1 Mark)**
Site selection is the most critical pre-camp decision. A poor site multiplies mortality, morbidity, and protection risks. Selection follows the **4-Filter Hierarchical Screening Model** recommended by Sphere and NDMA.

**2. Filter 1 — Safety (2 Marks)**
- The site **must not be in a flood-plain**, coastal high-hazard zone, or landslide-prone slope.
- Minimum elevation of **3 m above the Highest Flood Level (HFL)** is mandatory.
- Distance of **at least 500 m** from hazardous industries, electrical substations, and fuel stations.

**3. Filter 2 — Accessibility (1.5 Marks)**
- Approachable by an all-weather road that can carry **10-tonne relief trucks**.
- Provision of a **30 m × 30 m helipad** (day) and **50 m × 50 m (night)**.
- Proximity to a functional secondary/tertiary hospital within **10 km**.

**4. Filter 3 — Capacity (1.5 Marks)**
- Land must provide minimum **45 m² per person** (Sphere Standard).
- Topography should be **flat to gently sloping** (gradient ≤ 5%) for drainage.

**5. Filter 4 — Livelihoods & Dignity (1 Mark)**
- Distance from the affected village ≤ **5 km** to enable livelihood recovery.
- Cultural & gender considerations: separate WASH for women, space for religious congregations.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** A well-selected site reduces operational cost by 30–40% and ensures compliance with international humanitarian law.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — PART (b)

**1. Introduction (1 Mark)**
A relief camp is a **mini-city** with **6 functional zones** integrated into a single master plan.

**2. Six Functional Zones (5 Marks — 1 Mark each zone with % area)**

| Zone | % Area | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Accommodation | **40%** | Family tents / shelter units |
| Logistics | **25%** | Warehouse, helipad, parking, generator |
| Service | **10%** | Admin, registration, police post |
| WASH | **10%** | Water, toilets, bathing, laundry |
| Food & Nutrition | **10%** | Kitchen, warehouse, child feeding |
| Health | **5%** | OPD, IPD, isolation, psychosocial |

**3. Layout Principles (1 Mark)**
- Accommodation blocks must be **oriented along the wind direction** to allow cross-ventilation.
- Toilets must be **downwind and downstream** of the water points and accommodation.
- Helipad and generator must be **far from accommodation** to minimize noise and accident risk.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** A standardized 6-zone layout is the global benchmark and forms the basis of every Sphere-compliant camp.

---

### 📘 PART B — INTERNAL CHOICE (Alternative Question)

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Alternative Set]**
> **(a)** Describe the **minimum Sphere standards for Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene (WASH)** in a relief camp. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **camp management structure** and the role of the **Camp-In-Charge** with reference to the 6 functional cells. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — PART (a)

**1. Water Supply (2.5 Marks)**
- **15 L/person/day** minimum (Sphere Standard 2.1).
- Maximum distance to water point: **200 m** (Standard 2.2).
- Tap-stand to person ratio: **1 : 250** (Standard 2.2.3).
- Water quality must meet **WHO drinking-water guidelines** (E. coli = 0/100 mL).

**2. Sanitation – Toilets (2 Marks)**
- Toilet to person ratio: **1 : 20** (max).
- Distance from shelter to toilet: **≤ 50 m**.
- 50% of toilets to be **gender-separated**, with **1% accessible for persons with disabilities**.

**3. Hygiene (1.5 Marks)**
- Bathing cubicles: **1 : 50** persons.
- Laundry facility provided for every **100 persons**.
- Hygiene-promotion teams: **1 team per 1,000 persons**.

**4. Solid Waste & Vector Control (1 Mark)**
- Refuse pits **30 m away** from kitchens and water points.
- Daily vector control for **mosquitoes, flies, and rodents**.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** WASH failures are responsible for 60% of preventable deaths in camps — the Cholera outbreak in Gonaïves (Haiti 2010) post-earthquake illustrates this.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — PART (b)

**1. Camp Management Hierarchy (2 Marks)**
DDMA → Sub-Collector (SDM) → **Camp-In-Charge** → 6 Cell-Heads → Field staff.

**2. Role of the Camp-In-Charge (2.5 Marks)**
- Overall command and control of the camp.
- Conducting **daily morning briefings and evening debriefings**.
- Authorising resource allocation and inter-agency coordination.
- Submitting **daily situation reports (SITREP)** to the Sub-Collector.
- Acting as the **Complaints and Feedback Officer** for affected persons.

**3. Six Functional Cells (2.5 Marks)**
- **Shelter Cell** – Tent allocation, family tracing.
- **WASH Cell** – Water, sanitation, hygiene promotion.
- **Food Cell** – Ration distribution, kitchen management.
- **Health Cell** – OPD, IPD, isolation, mental-health first aid.
- **Protection Cell** – Women/child-friendly spaces, anti-trafficking measures.
- **Logistics Cell** – Warehousing, transport, communications.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** A well-coordinated camp is essentially a **miniature municipal corporation** running under disaster-time law.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in Relief-Camp Questions (Cost: 2–4 Marks Lost)**
>
> 1. **Numerical Errors** — Confusing **3.5 m²** (covered living area) with **45 m²** (total land area). They are **two different Sphere indicators** and examiners test this distinction.
> 2. **WASH Reversal** — Writing that *toilets should be upstream of water points* — **wrong**. They must be **downstream and downwind**.
> 3. **Forgetting Gender Cells** — In layout questions, the **Women-Friendly Space (WFS)** and **Child-Friendly Space (CFS)** are *mandatory* per IASC Guidelines, not optional.
> 4. **No Buffer Mention** — When computing quantities (water, food, tents), always add a **20% buffer** for pipeline failure and surge demand.
> 5. **Ignoring Camp-Closure Phase** — Many students write only about *establishment*. KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly values the **closure & transition phase** (Sphere Protection Principle 4).
> 6. **Citation Gap** — Always cite **Sphere 2018 / NDMP 2019 / IASC Guidelines** — a 0.5-mark “keyword-bonus” is given for proper referencing.

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **The 10 Most Important Things to Remember — Relief Camps (Module 4)**
>
> 1. **Relief camp = temporary planned settlement** for disaster-displaced populations.
> 2. **2 typologies** matter most: *Spontaneous* vs *Planned* (Kerala 2018 = Planned).
> 3. **Sphere Standards (2018)** are the *gold reference* — memorize the numerical values.
> 4. **Site selection** uses the 4-Filter model: **Safety, Accessibility, Capacity, Dignity**.
> 5. **45 m² per person** is the *total land standard*; **3.5 m²** is the *covered living area*.
> 6. **6 Functional Zones** mnemonic: **WAFHSL** (WASH, Accommodation, Food, Health, Service, Logistics).
> 7. **WASH numerics** — 15 L water, 1 toilet per 20, distance ≤ 200 m to water, ≤ 50 m to toilet.
> 8. **Food standard** — **2,100 kcal/person/day** minimum.
> 9. **Camp management** — DDMA → SDM → Camp-In-Charge → 6 Cell-Heads.
> 10. **Camp closure** must follow **Sphere Protection Principle 4**: "Avoid dependence, enable recovery."
>
> **Bonus Memory Hook:**
> - **15-3.5-45-20-50-200-2100** = the *7 sacred numbers* of any relief-camp numerical.

---
**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — RELIEF CAMPS**
*Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | PECET524 — Disaster Management*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET524 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Organization # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Disaster Management Organization (DMO):** The formalized, hierarchical, and multi-tiered institutional framework comprising governmental bodies, statutory authorities, specialized forces, and supporting agencies, mandated under the **Disaster Management Act, 2005** of India, to plan, coordinate, and execute preparedness, mitigation, response, and recovery operations before, during, and after a disaster event. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, "Organization" refers to the **institutional architecture** that governs disaster risk reduction (DRR) in India. It is a **three-tiered governmental structure** supplemented by specialized response forces, paramilitary units, community-level bodies, and international/NGOs. The three primary statutory bodies (defined under **Sections 3, 14, and 25** of the DM Act, 2005) are: | Tier | Body | Apex Head | |------|------|-----------| | **National** | National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) | Prime Minister of India | | **State** | State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA) | Chief Minister of the State | | **District** | District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA) | District Collector / District Magistrate | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Human Nervous System in an Emergency** Think of the disaster management organization like the **human body's nervous system during a sudden injury**: - **The Brain (NDMA)** — sits at the top (New Delhi), receives signals (situation reports), thinks strategically, and issues commands (policies, guidelines, directions). - **The Spinal Cord (SDMA)** — works at the state level. It receives commands from the brain and transmits reflexes down to the limbs, customizing the response to local anatomy (state-specific geography, population, vulnerabilities). - **The Reflexes & Limbs (DDMA)** — act at the grassroots. The moment a hot object touches the skin, the local nerves (DDMA) react *immediately*, while simultaneously signaling the spinal cord and brain for reinforcement. - **The Emergency Doctors (NDRF)** — specialized battalions of "paramedics" stationed at 16 locations across India, trained exclusively to handle the worst emergencies. - **The Local Helpers (Civil Defence, NCC, NSS, NGOs, PRIs)** — like bystanders and first-aid volunteers who assist the official response. Just as the body cannot function if the brain ignores the hand, the disaster management chain is **only as strong as the coordination between its tiers**. A weak link at any level (corrupt, untrained, or under-equipped) can cause the entire system to collapse during a real crisis. ## 1.3 Key Statutory Metrics & Standard Constants - **Disaster Management Act, 2005** — enacted on **23 December 2005**, notified on **9 January 2006**. - **NDMA** — first meeting held on **20 October 2006**. - **NDRF** — raised on **18 January 2006** with **8 battalions**, expanded to **16 battalions** by **2015** (each battalion = **1,149 personnel**, sanctioned strength). - **NIDM** — established in **1995** (as the National Centre for Disaster Management), upgraded and renamed in **2005**. - **India's risk profile** — among the world's most disaster-prone countries, with **27 of 29 states** and approximately **80% of the population** exposed to multiple hazards. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Tier Statutory Framework (DM Act, 2005) ### 2.1.1 National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) — Section 3 - **Chairperson:** Prime Minister of India. - **Members:** Maximum of **9 members**, who are ex-officio (Home Minister, Defence Minister, Finance Minister, etc., as notified). - **Term:** Members hold office for a term of **5 years** or until they hold the qualifying office, whichever is earlier. - **Functions:** - Lay down **policies, plans, and guidelines** for disaster management. - Approve the **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP)**. - Coordinate enforcement and implementation of policies. - Recommend provision of funds for mitigation projects. - Lay down guidelines for minimum standards of relief. - **Limitations:** NDMA is a **policy-making body**, *not an operational body* — it does not directly command troops or relief materials. ### 2.1.2 State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA) — Section 14 - **Chairperson:** Chief Minister of the concerned State. - **Members:** Up to **8 members** appointed by the State Cabinet. - **Functionality:** - Formulate **State Disaster Management Plan (SDMP)** in alignment with the NDMP. - Coordinate with the NDMA, the central ministries, and district authorities. - Ensure adequate preparedness, capacity-building, and training at the state level. - Provide funds to district authorities for disaster preparedness. - **Coordination body:** The State Executive Committee (SEC), headed by the **Chief Secretary**, handles the day-to-day operational aspects of SDMA. ### 2.1.3 District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA) — Section 25 - **Chairperson:** District Collector / District Magistrate (with a co-chairperson in case of a metropolitan city — the Municipal Commissioner). - **Members:** - Elected representative of the local authority (Chairperson of Zilla Parishad / Municipal body). - Superintendent of Police. - District-level officers from technical departments (Health, PWD, Agriculture). - Up to **2 experts** in disaster management nominated by the State Government. - **Functions:** - Prepare the **District Disaster Management Plan (DDMP)**. - Coordinate response and relief at the district level. - Ensure training and awareness programs at the **community level**. - Maintain a **District Disaster Management Resource Inventory**. - Activate the **Incident Response System (IRS)** at the disaster site. ## 2.2 Specialized Operational Bodies ### 2.2.1 National Disaster Response Force (NDRF) - **Raised:** 18 January 2006, under Section 44–45 of the DM Act, 2005. - **Administrative control:** Director General of Police (DGP) cadre, but **operational control** lies with the **NDMA**. - **Composition:** **16 battalions**, 8 drawn from the CRPF (Central Reserve Police Force) and 8 from the BSF (Border Security Force) — each battalion has **18 specialized search-and-rescue teams** (total ≈ **144 teams nationally**). - **Specializations:** Collapsed-structure search & rescue, swift-water rescue, CBRN (Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear) response, high-altitude operations, and parachute-trained commando units. - **Standard response time:** **15–20 minutes** from time of mobilization to deployment at the incident site. ### 2.2.2 National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM) - Apex **training and capacity-building** institution located in **New Delhi**. - Established in **1995** (as the National Centre for Disaster Management); reconstituted and renamed under the DM Act, 2005. - Functions: develop training modules, conduct research, publish resource materials, and organize national/international workshops. ### 2.2.3 Other Supporting Organizations | Organization | Role | |--------------|------| | **Indian Armed Forces (Army, Navy, Air Force)** | Major disaster response, HADR (Humanitarian Assistance and Disaster Relief) operations; integrated through the Integrated Defence Staff (IDS). | | **National Disaster Mitigation Fund & State Disaster Response Fund (SDRF)** | Financial backing — SDRF is funded with a 75:25 (Centre:State) ratio for most states, 90:10 for NE/Himalayan states. | | **National Disaster Management Programme (NDMP)** | Five-year strategic plan aligned with the Sendai Framework for DRR (2015–2030). | | **Incident Response System (IRS)** | Standardized on-site command structure; replaces the older "Crisis Management Group" model. | | **Local Bodies (PRIs / ULBs)** | Last-mile delivery of relief and community-based disaster risk reduction (CBDRR). | | **NDMA Volunteers (Aapda Mitra)** | Community volunteers trained in basic disaster response. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Disaster Management is a **qualitative, policy-based discipline**. Mathematical formulas are minimal; instead, the KTU exam focuses on **hierarchical structure, statutory provisions, and functional responsibilities**. The following "cheat sheet" summarizes all high-yield numerical/statutory data. | Parameter | Value / Standard | Governing Provision | |-----------|------------------|---------------------| | DM Act enacted | 23 December 2005 | Statute | | DM Act notified | 9 January 2006 | Gazette of India | | NDMA — max members | 9 (ex-officio) | Section 3(2) | | NDMA — term | 5 years | Section 3(6) | | SDMA — max members | 8 | Section 14(2) | | DDMA — chairperson | District Magistrate / Collector | Section 25(1) | | DDMA — co-chair (metro) | Municipal Commissioner | Section 25(1) proviso | | NDRF raised | 18 January 2006 | Section 44 | | NDRF — total battalions | 16 | Operational | | Each battalion strength | 1,149 personnel | Force structure | | NIDM — established | 1995 | Renamed 2005 | | SDRF funding (general states) | 75:25 (Centre:State) | Finance Commission recommendation | | SDRF funding (NE/Himalayan) | 90:10 | Finance Commission recommendation | | Sendai Framework period | 2015–2030 | UNDRR global policy | | NDRF standard mobilization time | 15–20 minutes | Internal SOP | > **Symbol Conventions Used in Equations (if any):** > $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value; $\mid$ denotes conditional probability (e.g., $P(A \mid B)$). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The organizational framework is **foundational for all disaster-resilient engineering projects**. Every Civil, Mechanical, and Electrical engineering project must align with the disaster plans issued by NDMA/SDMA/DDMA. For example: - **Civil Engineering** — Buildings in seismic zones must comply with **NDMA's Guidelines on Seismic Design** (BIS 1893, IS 4326). - **Urban Planning** — Smart city projects must align with DDMP hazard mapping and vulnerability assessments. - **Mechanical Engineering** — Equipment specifications for emergency vehicles (fire tenders, ambulances) follow BIS standards approved under the National Disaster Management Plan. - **Information Technology** — GIS-based decision support systems used by the NDMA require a clear chain of command to function effectively. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Disaster Management Organization is fundamentally a **policy/structural** topic with minimal numerical derivation. However, for KTU Board-level clarity, we present the **hierarchical derivation of the organizational structure** from first principles of the DM Act, 2005, and the **analytical matrix** of responsibilities across the three tiers. ## 3.1 Derivation of the Three-Tier Organizational Hierarchy The Indian DM organization is a **logical derivation from constitutional and administrative law principles**. The following stepwise derivation maps the rationale behind the three-tier structure: ### Step 1 — Identify the Constitutional Mandate India operates under a **federal, quasi-unitary governance model**. The **Constitution of India** (Article 73 — extent of executive power of the Union) and **Article 162** (state executive power) establish that disaster management is on the **Concurrent List (List III, Seventh Schedule)** — i.e., both Parliament and State Legislatures can legislate on it. This mandates **multi-tier governance**. ### Step 2 — Apply the Principle of Subsidiarity A fundamental tenet of disaster management is the **Principle of Subsidiarity**: *decision-making and response should occur at the lowest competent level*. $$D_{\text{decision}} = \min_{\text{level}} \left\{ L \;\middle|\; L \in \{\text{District}, \text{State}, \text{National}\} \right\}$$ This means a flood in Kuttanad (Kerala) should ideally be managed at the **District level** (DDMA), escalated to the **State level** (SDMA) only if the district capacity is overwhelmed, and further escalated to the **National level** (NDMA) only if multiple states are affected or the disaster is of national magnitude. ### Step 3 — Define the Apex Statutory Body (National Level) Section 3 of the DM Act, 2005, establishes **NDMA** as the apex policy body: $$\text{Authority}_{\text{National}} = \text{PM} \oplus \left\{ M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_9 \right\}_{\text{ex-officio}}$$ where $\oplus$ denotes the "chaired-by" relation, and $M_i$ are the 9 maximum members. The Prime Minister is a permanent ex-officio chair; the other 8 are ex-officio members. ### Step 4 — Mirror the National Structure at the State Level By the **principle of structural parallelism**, the State must have an analogous body to translate national policy into state action: $$\text{Authority}_{\text{State}} = \text{CM} \oplus \left\{ M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_8 \right\}_{\text{nominated}}$$ This is the **SDMA**, defined under Section 14 of the Act. The reduction from 9 to 8 members reflects the smaller scale of state-level operations. ### Step 5 — Establish the District-Level Operational Unit The district is the **basic unit of disaster response** in India. Section 25 establishes the **DDMA**: $$\text{Authority}_{\text{District}} = \text{DM} \oplus \left\{ \text{SP}, \text{ZP Chair}, \text{CEOs}, E_1, E_2 \right\}$$ where $E_1$ and $E_2$ are the 2 expert members. The DM (an IAS officer) acts as the **Incident Commander** in most events. ### Step 6 — Create the Specialized Response Force Recognizing that civilian authorities lack the technical capacity for high-risk response, Section 44 creates a **specialized force**: $$\text{NDRF} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{16} \text{Battalion}_i, \quad \text{where } \vert \text{Battalion}_i \vert = 1149$$ The first 8 battalions come from the CRPF, the next 8 from the BSF. Each battalion contains 18 specialized teams, yielding: $$T_{\text{total}} = 16 \times 18 = 288 \text{ teams}$$ ### Step 7 — Integrate the Training and Funding Mechanisms To sustain the system: - **Training** $\rightarrow$ NIDM (National Institute of Disaster Management). - **Funding** $\rightarrow$ SDRF (State Disaster Response Fund) + NDMF (National Disaster Mitigation Fund) + NCCF (National Cyclone Risk Mitigation Project). The complete organization tree can therefore be expressed as: $$\text{DM Organization} = \text{NDMA} \cup \text{SDMA}_{s=1}^{29} \cup \text{DDMA}_{d=1}^{N} \cup \text{NDRF} \cup \text{NIDM} \cup \text{Military} \cup \text{NGOs}$$ where $N$ is the total number of districts in India ($N \approx 766$ as of recent reorganization). ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Responsibility Allocation The following matrix unambiguously maps the responsibility for each phase of disaster management to the organizational tier. This is a **high-yield KTU exam matrix** — questions on this table appear in almost every board examination. | Disaster Phase | Primary Responsibility | Supporting Body | Legal Mandate | |----------------|------------------------|-----------------|---------------| | **Pre-Disaster (Preparedness)** | NDMA (national policy) / SDMA (state plan) / DDMA (district plan) | NIDM, Armed Forces, NDRF, PRIs | Sec 18, 31, 37 | | **Mitigation** | NDMA / State Departments / DDMA | BMTPC, BIS, HUDCO | Sec 6(2)(h), Sec 18 | | **Capacity Building & Training** | NIDM / ATI (State Admin. Training Institutes) | SDMA, DDMA | Sec 42, 60 | | **Early Warning** | IMD / INCOIS / CWC | SDMA, DDMA, Media | Sec 6(2)(b) | | **Response (during disaster)** | SDMA / DDMA | NDRF, Army, SDRF, NGOs | Sec 14, 25, 44 | | **Evacuation** | DDMA | NDRF, Police, Civil Defence | Sec 25(2)(d) | | **Relief Distribution** | DDMA / District Administration | SDMA, Red Cross, NGOs | Sec 19, 32 | | **Recovery & Rehabilitation** | SDMA / State Departments | World Bank, ADB, NDMA | Sec 18(2)(j) | | **Damage & Needs Assessment** | DDMA (primary) → SDMA → NDMA | UNDP, UN agencies | Sec 18(2)(d) | | **Post-Disaster Reconstruction** | State PWD / Housing Board | World Bank, NDMA | Sec 14 | ## 3.3 Hierarchical Command Flow — Analytical Walkthrough A real-world example, the **2018 Kerala Floods**, demonstrates the command flow: 1. **Day 0:** IMD issues red alert → Kerala SDMA activates the **State Emergency Operation Centre (SEOC)**. 2. **Day 1:** Idukki district is inundated → DDMA Idukki activates the **District Emergency Operation Centre (DEOC)**. 3. **Day 2:** Multiple districts affected → SDMA requests NDRF teams from Arakkonam (Tamil Nadu) and Pune. 4. **Day 3:** The Chief Minister of Kerala requests the **Indian Air Force (IAF)** for helicopter rescue — a request routed through the **Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA)** because defence assets are federal. 5. **Day 4:** NDMA convenes an emergency meeting → announces **"severe nature"** of the disaster → triggers national-level funding. 6. **Day 7:** International aid offers begin — routed through the **Ministry of External Affairs (MEA)** under **NDMA guidelines**. 7. **Post-flood:** SDMA submits **Memorandum of Damage** → Centre releases **SDRF and additional assistance**. The **lesson for KTU exams** is that the *chain of command flows upward for assistance requests, and downward for orders/directives* — this is the "asymmetric" structure of India's DM organization. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the roles of **NDMA, NDRF, and NCCF**. Remember: NDMA is the **policy/think-tank** body; NDRF is the **operational rescue force**; NCCF is a **funding project** for cyclone mitigation. Mixing these is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in ESE. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Three-Tier Organizational Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[PM OF INDIA
CHAIRPERSON] --> nodeB[NDMA
9 MEMBERS
POLICY BODY] nodeA --> nodeC[MINISTRY OF HOME AFFAIRS] nodeC --> nodeD[SECRETARIAT OF NDMA] nodeB --> nodeE[CM OF STATE
CHAIRPERSON] nodeE --> nodeF[SDMA
8 MEMBERS
STATE LEVEL] nodeE --> nodeG[STATE EXECUTIVE
COMMITTEE
CHIEF SECRETARY HEADS] nodeF --> nodeH[DM DISTRICT COLLECTOR
CHAIRPERSON] nodeH --> nodeI[DDMA
DISTRICT LEVEL] nodeH --> nodeJ[CO-CHAIR
MUNICIPAL COMMISSIONER] nodeI --> nodeK[TEHSIL TALUK LEVEL
SUB DIVISIONAL AUTHORITY] nodeI --> nodeL[BLOCK LEVEL
LOCAL ADMINISTRATION] nodeI --> nodeM[GRAM PANCHAYAT
COMMUNITY LEVEL] nodeB --> nodeN[NDRF 16 BATTALIONS] nodeB --> nodeO[NIDM NEW DELHI] nodeB --> nodeP[ARMED FORCES ARMY NAVY IAF] classDef apex fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff classDef state fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff classDef district fill:#15803d,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff classDef support fill:#a16207,stroke:#a16207,color:#ffffff class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD apex class nodeE,nodeF,nodeG state class nodeH,nodeI,nodeJ,nodeK,nodeL,nodeM district class nodeN,nodeO,nodeP support ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Command Flow During a Disaster ```mermaid graph TD event1[HAZARD OCCURS
EARTHQUAKE FLOOD CYCLONE] --> stage1[IMD INCOIS ISSUES WARNING] stage1 --> stage2[NDMA RECEIVES DATA] stage2 --> stage3[SDMA NOTIFIED] stage2 --> stage4[NDRF PUT ON ALERT] stage3 --> stage5[DDMA ACTIVATES IRS] stage5 --> stage6[DEOC DISTRICT EMERGENCY
OPERATION CENTRE] stage4 --> stage7[NDRF DEPLOYED

SEARCH RESCUE] stage7 --> stage9 stage9 --> stage10[RELIEF CAMPS] stage9 --> stage11[TRIAGE MEDICAL RESPONSE] stage9 --> stage12[NEEDS ASSESSMENT] stage10 --> stage13[SDMA COORDINATES
STATE RESOURCES] stage11 --> stage13 stage12 --> stage13 stage13 --> stage14[NDMA COORDINATES

REHABILITATION] stage15 --> stage16[POST DISASTER

OF INDIA] c2 --> c3[SDRF
