# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Food Grain Requirement for a Disaster Zone

### Problem Setup
A district of **1,00,000** population is hit by a **flash flood**. Standard relief norms prescribe a **7-day emergency ration** at the Sphere minimum of **2,100 Kcal/person/day**, with **rice** as the primary cereal (energy density = **3,500 Kcal/kg of raw rice**, with 90% effective yield after cooking losses). A **15% safety margin** must be added for spoilage, transit loss, and double-counting.

**Goal:** Compute the total tonnage of raw rice required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the demographic constant.**
The per capita daily energy norm is given by the Sphere Standard:
$$
C_n = 2100 \text{ Kcal/person/day}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the total daily energy demand of the affected population.**
$$
E_{daily} = P \times C_n
$$
Substituting $P = 100{,}000$:
$$
E_{daily} = 100{,}000 \times 2100
$$
$$
E_{daily} = 2.1 \times 10^{8} \text{ Kcal/day}
$$

**Step 3: Convert daily energy demand to daily grain requirement.**
The energy density of raw rice is $E_{cereal} = 3500$ Kcal/kg. So the daily raw rice need (before processing loss) is:
$$
G_{daily} = \frac{E_{daily}}{E_{cereal}}
$$
$$
G_{daily} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{8}}{3500}
$$
$$
G_{daily} = 60{,}000 \text{ kg/day} = 60 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the disaster duration factor (7 days).**
$$
G_{total\,(raw)} = G_{daily} \times D
$$
$$
G_{total\,(raw)} = 60{,}000 \times 7 = 4{,}20{,}000 \text{ kg} = 420 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the safety margin $s = 0.15$.**
$$
R_{ration} = G_{total\,(raw)} \times (1 + s)
$$
$$
R_{ration} = 420 \times 1.15
$$
$$
R_{ration} = 483 \text{ tonnes of raw rice}
$$

### Final Result
$$
\boxed{R_{ration} = 483 \text{ tonnes of raw rice for 7 days}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:**
> People → Energy (×Kcal) → Grain (÷Kcal/kg) → Duration (×days) → Safety (×1.15)

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Calorie Norms by Demographic Group

| Group | Weight $w_i$ (%) | Calorie Norm $c_i$ (Kcal/day) | Protein (g/day) | Special Needs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adult Male (sedentary) | 30 | 2400 | 60 | — |
| Adult Female (sedentary) | 30 | 1900 | 55 | — |
| Children (6–14 yrs) | 20 | 2100 | 50 | Vitamin A |
| Children (0–6 yrs) | 8 | 1200 | 25 | ICDS ration |
| Pregnant/Lactating Women | 7 | 2700 | 78 | Iron, Folic Acid |
| Elderly (60+ yrs) | 5 | 1800 | 50 | Soft diet |
| **Weighted Average $\bar{C}$** | 100 | **~2100** | — | — |

### Derivation of Weighted Average
$$
\bar{C} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot c_i
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{C} &= (0.30)(2400) + (0.30)(1900) + (0.20)(2100) \\
&\quad + (0.08)(1200) + (0.07)(2700) + (0.05)(1800)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{C} &= 720 + 570 + 420 + 96 + 189 + 90
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{C} = 2085 \text{ Kcal/person/day} \approx 2100 \text{ Kcal/person/day}}
$$

This is the **engineering justification** for using 2100 Kcal as the standard disaster relief norm.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: National Buffer Stock Requirement

The FCI maintains a strategic buffer to meet emergency and Price Support Operations. A simplified derivation:

**Given:**
- National population: $P_N = 140 \times 10^7$
- Per capita monthly grain need: $m = 13$ kg
- Buffer holding period: $T = 3$ months

**Derivation:**
$$
S_{buffer} = P_N \times m \times T
$$
$$
S_{buffer} = (1.4 \times 10^9) \times 13 \times 3
$$
$$
S_{buffer} = 5.46 \times 10^{10} \text{ kg}
$$
$$
S_{buffer} = 546 \text{ Lakh Tonnes (LT)} = 54.6 \text{ Million Tonnes (MT)}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{buffer} \approx 55 \text{ MT for 3-month national security}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **theoretical maximum**; actual FCI norms are 21.04 MT (as on 1st April) to 70.31 MT (as on 1st July) for food security, due to seasonal procurement cycles.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Drought Relief — Maharashtra-Type Scenario

**Scenario:** A drought in 8 districts affects 50 lakh people. The state government must provide a **30-day dry ration** comprising **rice (60%), wheat (30%), and pulses (10%)** with a **20% safety margin**.

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1:** Daily energy demand:
$$
E_{daily} = 5 \times 10^6 \times 2100 = 1.05 \times 10^{10} \text{ Kcal/day}
$$

**Step 2:** Total 30-day demand:
$$
E_{30} = 1.05 \times 10^{10} \times 30 = 3.15 \times 10^{11} \text{ Kcal}
$$

**Step 3:** Total grain mass (using 3500 Kcal/kg):
$$
G = \frac{3.15 \times 10^{11}}{3500} = 9.0 \times 10^7 \text{ kg} = 90{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply safety margin $s = 0.20$:
$$
G_{final} = 90{,}000 \times 1.20 = 1{,}08{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 5:** Split by composition:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rice (60%)} &: 64{,}800 \text{ tonnes} \\
\text{Wheat (30%)} &: 32{,}400 \text{ tonnes} \\
\text{Pulses (10%)} &: 10{,}800 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Ration} = 1{,}08{,}000 \text{ tonnes for 30 days}}
$$

## 4.1 Food Requirement Planning Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram illustrates the **decision flow** from disaster onset to final ration distribution, mapping the functional blocks used by district and state disaster management authorities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Disaster Onset] --> B[Damage and Needs Assessment DNA]
    B --> C[Demographic Mapping]
    C --> D[Caloric Norm Selection]
    D --> E[Total Energy Demand Calculation]
    E --> F[Inventory Check FCI and State Godowns]
    F --> G{Buffer Sufficient?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Direct Mobilization]
    G -- No --> I[Open Market Procurement]
    I --> J[Central Government Allocation]
    H --> K[Transport and Logistics]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Relief Camps and PDS Outlets]
    L --> M[Community Kitchens]
    L --> N[Dry Ration Distribution]
    M --> O[Monitoring and Surveillance]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Feedback Loop to DNA]
    P --> B
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Level 1 Energy Staples 60 percent of ration Rice Wheat Maize]
    L2[Level 2 Protein Sources 20 percent Pulses Legumes Eggs if available]
    L3[Level 3 Protective Foods 10 percent Vegetables Fruits locally sourced]
    L4[Level 4 Fats and Oils 5 percent Cooking oil Ghee]
    L5[Level 5 Micronutrients 5 percent Iodized salt Vitamin A Iron supplements]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

## 4.3 Institutional Framework for Food Security in India

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Ministry of Consumer Affairs Food and PD] --> B[FCI Food Corporation of India]
    A --> C[DFPD Department of Food and PD]
    B --> D[Central Godowns]
    C --> E[State Civil Supplies]
    E --> F[District Supply Office]
    F --> G[Fair Price Shop FPS]
    G --> H[Ration Card Holder Beneficiary]
    H --> I[Vulnerable Groups PLW Children Elderly]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Block 1 (4.1)** shows the closed-loop feedback system — DNA is not a one-time event; it repeats as the situation evolves.
> - **Block 2 (4.2)** shows the **nutritional pyramid**; KTU examiners frequently test the 60-20-10-5-5 ratio.
> - **Block 3 (4.3)** shows the **vertical hierarchy** of India's food security architecture from Centre to Beneficiary.

flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Acute 0 to 7 days] --> S1[Ready to eat meals Community kitchens]
    P2[Phase 2 Stabilization 8 to 30 days] --> S2[Dry rations Fortified flour]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define "Food Requirement" in the context of disaster management. List the **Sphere Standards** for minimum daily food ration in disaster response.

**Model Answer:**

**Food Requirement** in disaster management refers to the systematic estimation, procurement, and distribution of nutritionally adequate food to the disaster-affected population, ensuring survival, health, and dignity during the emergency and recovery phases.

**Sphere Standards (2018) for minimum daily food ration:**
- **Energy:** Minimum **2,100 Kcal/person/day**
- **Protein:** At least **10%** of total energy from protein (≈ 52 g/day)
- **Fat:** At least **17%** of total energy from fat
- **Micronutrients:** Adequate intake of Vitamin A, Iron, Iodine
- **Water:** **2.5–3 L/person/day** (drinking) + **2 L/person/day** (basic hygiene)

---

### Question 2: What is **Buffer Stock**? Mention the current FCI norms for wheat and rice buffer stock as on 1st April and 1st July.

**Model Answer:**

**Buffer Stock** is the strategic reserve of food grains (wheat and rice) maintained by the **Food Corporation of India (FCI)** on behalf of the Government of India to:
1. Meet statutory requirements under the **National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013**
2. Address emergencies and natural disasters
3. Stabilize open market prices
4. Support **Price Support Operations (PSO)** for farmers

**FCI Buffer Norms:**

| Date | Wheat (LMT) | Rice (LMT) | Total (LMT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st April | 7.46 | 13.58 | 21.04 |
| 1st July | 27.58 | 27.73 | 55.31 |
| 1st October | 20.52 | 16.71 | 37.23 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Buffer Stock** (strategic reserve for emergencies) with **Operational Stock** (stock-in-transit + stock in godowns for PDS distribution). They are **not the same**. Buffer stock is fixed by government policy; operational stock is variable.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A — Choice 1**

### Q. A(a) [7 Marks] — Discuss the demographic and physiological factors that determine the food requirement of a disaster-affected population. How does the **National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013** ensure food security during disasters?

**Model Solution:**

**1. Demographic Factors Determining Food Requirement:**

- **Total Affected Population:** Estimated through door-to-door survey, satellite imagery, and crowd-sourced data.
- **Age-Sex Distribution:** Calorie norms vary sharply — adult male (2,400 Kcal) vs. child 0–6 (1,200 Kcal) vs. pregnant women (2,700 Kcal).
- **Vulnerable Groups:** Pregnant & Lactating Women (PLW), children under 6, elderly, and disabled persons require **enhanced rations**.
- **Pre-existing Nutritional Status:** Areas with chronic malnutrition (e.g., tribal belts) need fortified rations.
- **Displacement Status:** Displaced populations in camps require **community kitchens**; partially displaced need **dry rations**.

**2. Physiological Factors:**

- **Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR):** Higher in cold climates and among manual laborers.
- **Climate & Ambient Temperature:** Heat stress increases calorie and water needs.
- **Disease Burden:** Diarrheal outbreaks increase fluid and electrolyte loss.
- **Physical Activity:** Rescue/cleanup workers need up to **3,500 Kcal/day**.

**3. Role of NFSA 2013 in Disaster Food Security:**

The **National Food Security Act, 2013** is a landmark legislation that legally entitles **67% of India's population** to subsidized food grains. Its disaster-relevant provisions are:

- **Coverage:** 75% of rural and 50% of urban population (≈ 81.35 crore persons).
- **Entitlement:** **5 kg per person per month** of foodgrains at **₹1/kg (millet), ₹2/kg (wheat), ₹3/kg (rice)**.
- **Pregnant & Lactating Women:** Entitled to **600 Kcal of free meal** through ICDS + ₹6,000 maternity benefit.
- **Migrant Families:** NFSA mandates **portability of ration cards** through **One Nation One Ration Card (ONORC)** scheme — critical during displacement.
- **Disaster Clause:** Section 42 mandates that in case of natural calamities, the State Government shall provide **food security to all affected persons without any discrimination**, beyond the regular PDS quota.
- **Grievance Redressal:** District Grievance Redressal Officer (DGRO) must address food-related complaints within **30 days**.

### Q. A(b) [7 Marks] — A cyclone has displaced **2,00,000** people in a coastal district. The relief standard is **2,100 Kcal/person/day** for **10 days**. If the ration mix is **70% rice** and **30% wheat**, calculate the total requirement of each grain in tonnes, assuming a **15% safety margin** and an energy density of **3,500 Kcal/kg** for rice and **3,400 Kcal/kg** for wheat.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $P = 2{,}00{,}000$ persons
- $C_n = 2100$ Kcal/person/day
- $D = 10$ days
- Safety margin $s = 0.15$
- Rice mix: 70% of total calories
- Wheat mix: 30% of total calories
- $E_{rice} = 3500$ Kcal/kg, $E_{wheat} = 3400$ Kcal/kg

**Step 1: Total Energy Demand (10 days)**
$$
E_{total} = P \times C_n \times D
$$
$$
E_{total} = 2 \times 10^5 \times 2100 \times 10
$$
$$
E_{total} = 4.2 \times 10^9 \text{ Kcal}
$$

**Step 2: Calorie Allocation**
$$
E_{rice} = 0.70 \times 4.2 \times 10^9 = 2.94 \times 10^9 \text{ Kcal}
$$
$$
E_{wheat} = 0.30 \times 4.2 \times 10^9 = 1.26 \times 10^9 \text{ Kcal}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to Grain Mass (kg)**
$$
M_{rice} = \frac{2.94 \times 10^9}{3500} = 8.4 \times 10^5 \text{ kg} = 840 \text{ tonnes}
$$
$$
M_{wheat} = \frac{1.26 \times 10^9}{3400} = 3.706 \times 10^5 \text{ kg} \approx 370.6 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Safety Margin**
$$
M_{rice,\,final} = 840 \times 1.15 = 966 \text{ tonnes}
$$
$$
M_{wheat,\,final} = 370.6 \times 1.15 \approx 426.2 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{M_{rice} = 966 \text{ tonnes}, \quad M_{wheat} = 426.2 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 14-Mark Numerical:** Students frequently make the following errors:
> 1. **Forgetting the safety margin** (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Using the same energy density for rice and wheat** (wheat is ~3,400 vs rice ~3,500 Kcal/kg).
> 3. **Mixing up the mix ratio** — 70% means 70% of *calories*, not 70% of *mass*.
> 4. **Unit conversion errors** — 1 tonne = 1,000 kg; forgetting this costs 1 mark.

---

> **Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

### Q. B(a) [7 Marks] — Explain the **institutional framework** for food security in India. Discuss the role of **FCI**, **PDS**, **ICDS**, and **Mid-Day Meal Scheme** in disaster food management.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Institutional Framework — Vertical Hierarchy:**

| Level | Institution | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Central | Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food & PD | Policy & procurement |
| Central | FCI (Food Corporation of India) | Procurement, storage, distribution |
| Central | DFPD | NFSA implementation |
| State | State Civil Supplies Corp. | State-level PDS |
| District | District Supply Office | FPS allotment, monitoring |
| Local | Fair Price Shop (FPS) | Last-mile distribution |
| Local | Anganwadi Centre | ICDS supplementary nutrition |

**2. Role of FCI in Disaster Food Management:**
- Maintains **strategic buffer stock** (21–70 LMT as per seasonal norms).
- **Custom-milled** rice/wheat for relief kits.
- Operates **mobile godowns** in flood-affected states (e.g., Bihar, Assam).
- Coordinates with **Indian Railways** for rapid grain transport (e.g., during 2013 Uttarakhand floods).

**3. Role of PDS:**
- Operates **5.4 lakh Fair Price Shops** across India.
- Distributes **NFSA-mandated 5 kg/person/month** at subsidized rates.
- **One Nation One Ration Card (ONORC)** allows migrant families to access PDS anywhere.
- Acts as the **first-line delivery channel** for disaster rations.

**4. Role of ICDS:**
- Reaches **8 crore children** through **13.9 lakh Anganwadi Centres**.
- Provides **Take-Home Ration (THR)** to PLW and children.
- Critical in **drought** and **flood** situations for **supplementary feeding**.

**5. Role of Mid-Day Meal (PM POSHAN) Scheme:**
- Covers **11.80 crore children** in government schools.
- Acts as a **nutrition safety net** when schools reopen post-disaster.
- Provides **cooked meals** with 450–700 Kcal/meal depending on age group.

### Q. B(b) [7 Marks] — A drought-affected region has a population of **50,000**. Calculate the **monthly food grain requirement** under NFSA norms (5 kg/person/month), the **caloric value** of this ration (assuming all rice at 3,500 Kcal/kg), and the **total cost** to the government at **₹3/kg of rice**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P = 50{,}000$
- NFSA entitlement: $m = 5$ kg/person/month
- $E_{rice} = 3500$ Kcal/kg
- Subsidy price: $r = ₹3$/kg

**Step 1: Total Monthly Grain Requirement**
$$
G = P \times m
$$
$$
G = 50{,}000 \times 5 = 2{,}50{,}000 \text{ kg} = 250 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 2: Total Monthly Caloric Value**
$$
E = G \times E_{rice}
$$
$$
E = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 3500 = 8.75 \times 10^8 \text{ Kcal}
$$

**Step 3: Per Capita Daily Calorie from NFSA**
$$
C_{NFSA} = \frac{E}{P \times 30} = \frac{8.75 \times 10^8}{50{,}000 \times 30}
$$
$$
C_{NFSA} = \frac{8.75 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 583.3 \text{ Kcal/person/day}
$$

**Step 4: Total Government Subsidy Cost**
$$
\text{Cost} = G \times r
$$
$$
\text{Cost} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 3 = ₹7{,}50{,}000
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{G = 250 \text{ tonnes}, \quad C_{NFSA} \approx 583 \text{ Kcal/person/day}, \quad \text{Cost} = ₹7.5 \text{ lakh}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight (for 7-mark questions):** The NFSA entitlement of 5 kg/person/month yields only **~583 Kcal/day** — far below the 2,100 Kcal Sphere Standard. Therefore, **NFSA is a safety net, not a disaster relief measure.** Disaster-affected populations need **additional emergency rations** from SDRF, NDRF, or state calamity funds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Sphere Standard:** Minimum **2,100 Kcal/person/day** in disaster response.
- ✅ **NITI Aayog Norm:** Rural 2,400 Kcal, Urban 2,100 Kcal.
- ✅ **ICMR Protein Norm:** 60 g/day (adult), 78 g/day (PLW).
- ✅ **Water Need:** 3 L/day (drinking) + 2 L/day (hygiene) per person.
- ✅ **NFSA Entitlement:** 5 kg/person/month at ₹1/2/3 per kg.
- ✅ **FCI Buffer Stock Range:** 21.04 LMT (April) to 70.31 LMT (July).
- ✅ **Energy Density:** Rice ≈ 3,500 Kcal/kg; Wheat ≈ 3,400 Kcal/kg.
- ✅ **Safety Margin:** Always add **15–20%** in disaster calculations.
- ✅ **Vulnerable Groups:** PLW, Children 0–6, Elderly, Disabled — need **higher allocations**.
- ✅ **Institutional Pillars:** FCI (procurement) + PDS (distribution) + ICDS (nutrition) + PM POSHAN (school meals).
- ✅ **Phase-wise Strategy:** Phase 1 (Ready-to-Eat) → Phase 2 (Dry Rations) → Phase 3 (PDS Restoration) → Phase 4 (Rehabilitation).
- ✅ **ONORC:** Ration card portability for migrant families during disasters.
- ✅ **Disaster Ration Formula:** $R_{ration} = P \times C_n \times D \times (1+s) \div E_{cereal}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Exam Traps to Avoid:**
> 1. Confusing **buffer stock** with **operational stock**.
> 2. Forgetting the **15–20% safety margin** in numerical problems.
> 3. Assuming **rice and wheat have identical** energy density.
> 4. Treating **NFSA as a disaster relief** — it is not; it's a baseline entitlement.
> 5. Missing the **role of vulnerable groups** (PLW, children) in demographic weighting.

> **End of Module 4 — Topic: Food Requirement | PECET524 | Disaster Management**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Needs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET524 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Needs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Water Needs** in disaster management refer to the **quantitative and qualitative requirements of potable and non-potable water** essential for sustaining human life, public health, livestock survival, and basic community functioning during the **emergency, stabilization, and recovery phases** of a disaster. It encompasses per-capita consumption norms, minimum survival thresholds, hygienic thresholds, and institutional/livestock demands as codified under the **Sphere Standards (2018 Edition)** and the **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality** in emergency situations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Water Needs" must NOT be confused with routine municipal water supply design. In a disaster context, the focus is on **survival thresholds**, **rapid-deployable quantities**, and **source reliability under compromised infrastructure** — a fundamentally different engineering problem.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (The "Real-World Analogy")

Imagine a large office canteen kitchen that suddenly loses its municipal pipeline connection due to a city-wide pipe burst. The canteen manager does not ask, *"How much water does an office need in a month?"* — the manager asks three urgent questions:

1. **"How much can a person survive on?"** → The **survival minimum** (drinking + cooking).
2. **"How much keeps a person from getting sick?"** → The **hygiene minimum** (washing, sanitation).
3. **"How much allows a hospital ward to function?"** → The **institutional minimum** (much higher).

**Water Needs in disaster management is exactly this logic — applied to a city, a relief camp, or a flooded village.** The engineer must size water tankers, storage tanks, and treatment units based on the *minimum threshold that prevents disease outbreak and death*, not on the comfort-level of normal life.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Anchors (Memorize These)

The following are internationally accepted benchmark values:

| Anchor | Value | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolute survival (drinking only)** | **3 L per person per day** | WHO Emergency Standard |
| **Minimum for survival (drink + cook + basic hygiene)** | **7.5 L per person per day** | Sphere Standard |
| **Recommended for full hygiene and basic needs** | **15 L per person per day** | Sphere Standard |
| **Average for full domestic use (disaster response)** | **20–25 L per person per day** | NDMA / Government of India |
| **Hospital in-patient** | **100 L per patient per day** | Sphere Standard |
| **Cattle / large livestock** | **30–50 L per animal per day** | NDMA Guidelines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** The *minimum* (3 L) is the **survival threshold** — below this, dehydration deaths occur. The *recommended* (15 L) is the **health-maintaining threshold** — below this, disease outbreaks (cholera, typhoid) become statistically likely. The KTU examiner will test whether you know **which figure to use in which phase of disaster response**.

## 1.4 Phases of Disaster Where Water Needs Differ

The same population has **different water needs at different times** after a disaster strikes:

- **Phase 1 — Emergency (0–72 hours):** Survival focus. ~3–7.5 L/p/d. Sources: bottled water, tankers.
- **Phase 2 — Stabilization (3 days – 1 month):** Health protection. ~15 L/p/d. Sources: tankers + temporary piped networks.
- **Phase 3 — Recovery (1–6 months):** Normalization. ~20–25 L/p/d. Sources: restored infrastructure + supplemented supplies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Composition of Water Needs

In disaster scenarios, total water demand of a relief camp or affected community is the **algebraic sum of distinct demand categories**. This decomposition is the foundation of every KTU numerical problem on this topic.

### Step-by-Step Logic (Why each step exists)

1. **Drinking Water Demand (D_d):** Humans cannot survive without it. Biochemically, an adult loses ~2.5 L/day through respiration, perspiration, and urine. Above **3 L/p/d**, mortality from dehydration drops sharply.
2. **Cooking Water Demand (D_c):** Required for rehydration of food, washing utensils, and basic kitchen hygiene. Standard value: **2–3 L/p/d** for a cereal-based relief ration.
3. **Personal Hygiene Demand (D_h):** Handwashing, body cleaning, brushing. Critical for preventing fecal-oral disease transmission. Standard value: **4–6 L/p/d** at the Sphere minimum.
4. **Laundry Demand (D_l):** Washing clothes, bedding. Often **dropped or rationed** in early emergency phase to focus on drinking/hygiene.
5. **Cleaning of Shelter/Toilets (D_s):** Sanitation-linked. Sphere recommends 2–6 L/p/d for this category.
6. **Institutional Demand (D_i):** Hospitals, feeding centers, schools-as-shelters consume disproportionately more water (often 5–10× per capita).
7. **Livestock Demand (D_L):** Cattle, goats, and draught animals cannot be neglected — failure to water them causes economic collapse of the affected community and loss of milk supply.

The total water demand is therefore:

$$\boxed{W_{total} = P \cdot (D_d + D_c + D_h + D_l + D_s) + W_i + W_L}$$

Where:
- $P$ = total affected population
- $W_i$ = total institutional water demand (L/day)
- $W_L$ = total livestock water demand (L/day)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All quantities in Litres (L) unless stated. Convert $\text{m}^3 = \text{kL}$ to L by multiplying by 1000. $1 \text{ gallon (Imperial)} \approx 4.546 \text{ L}$.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = P \cdot q$ | Total water demand = population × per-capita rate | L/day |
| 2 | $V_{tank} = \dfrac{P \cdot q \cdot n}{1000}$ | Required storage tank volume (P × per-capita × storage days) | m³ |
| 3 | $N_{tanker} = \dfrac{P \cdot q \cdot T}{C_{tanker} \cdot f}$ | Number of tankers needed per day (P × per-capita × days ÷ tanker capacity ÷ trips) | tankers/day |
| 4 | $W_{livestock} = \sum (N_k \cdot q_k)$ | Total livestock water demand (sum of species × heads × per-animal rate) | L/day |
| 5 | $W_{hospital} = N_{beds} \cdot 100$ | Hospital water demand (Sphere: 100 L/patient/day) | L/day |
| 6 | $f_{peak} = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0$ | Peak factor multiplier (morning peak demand in camps) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $A_{chlorine} = \dfrac{Q \cdot D}{C \cdot 10}$ | Bleach dose (Q = flow in L/s, D = desired dose mg/L, C = chlorine % in solution) | mL/min |
| 8 | $V_{bladder} = P \cdot q \cdot t_h$ | Hourly demand for pump/bladder sizing | L/hour |

**Where:**
- $P$ = population to be served
- $q$ = per-capita water demand (L/p/d)
- $n$ = number of storage days (1–3 days typical)
- $T$ = planning period (days)
- $C_{tanker}$ = capacity of one tanker truck (typically **5000 L to 12000 L**)
- $f$ = number of trips per tanker per day (typically 2–3)
- $N_k$ = number of animals of species $k$
- $q_k$ = per-animal demand of species $k$
- $t_h$ = hours of peak demand (typically 2–3 hours in morning)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above framework is used by:
- **NDMA / SDMA** for sizing relief-camp infrastructure.
- **Indian Army / NDRF** for calculating tanker convoys during flood operations.
- **Public Health Engineering Departments** for designing emergency water-supply schemes (hand-pump + bladder tank + tanker combinations).
- **UNICEF / Red Cross** in international humanitarian operations (Sphere compliance audits).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In KTU problems, students often confuse *total water need* with *drinking water need*. The exam will deliberately give a small per-capita number (e.g., 3 L) and ask for "total water need" — you must recognize that this refers only to drinking, and total demand requires the full 15–20 L/p/d.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Tanker Fleet Size for an Emergency

A relief agency must supply water to **P people** for **T days**, with each tanker of capacity $C$ litres making **f trips/day**. Derive the required number of tankers $N$.

**Step 1 — Total water requirement over the planning period:**

The total volume of water to be delivered is the population multiplied by the per-capita daily demand multiplied by the planning period.

$$W_{total} = P \cdot q \cdot T \quad \text{[L over the period]}$$

**Step 2 — Water delivered by one tanker per day:**

One tanker makes $f$ trips per day, each trip carrying $C$ litres. Therefore the daily delivery per tanker is the product of capacity, trips, and days.

$$W_{1\text{ tanker}} = C \cdot f \quad \text{[L/day per tanker]}$$

**Step 3 — Equating demand to supply over the full period:**

The total water delivered by $N$ tankers over $T$ days must equal the total water requirement. We solve for $N$ by dividing total demand by per-tanker total delivery.

$$N \cdot C \cdot f \cdot T = P \cdot q \cdot T$$

The $T$ cancels from both sides (because both sides scale with the same planning period).

$$N = \dfrac{P \cdot q}{C \cdot f}$$

**Step 4 — Generalization with safety/peak factor:**

In practice, a peak factor $f_p$ is applied to handle morning concentration of demand and unforeseen wastage.

$$\boxed{N_{tanker} = \dfrac{P \cdot q \cdot f_p}{C \cdot f}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Storage Tank Capacity for a Relief Camp

A camp of $P$ people must store enough water to survive an interruption of **n days**, with average per-capita demand $q$.

**Step 1 — Daily demand:**

The daily water demand of the camp is the product of population and per-capita consumption.

$$Q_{daily} = P \cdot q \quad \text{[L/day]}$$

**Step 2 — Total storage required:**

The total storage volume in litres is the daily demand multiplied by the number of days of autonomy required. This represents the volume the tank must physically hold.

$$V_{litres} = P \cdot q \cdot n$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to cubic metres (engineering standard unit):**

Engineers design storage tanks in cubic metres because $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$. We divide the litre volume by 1000 to obtain the tank capacity in cubic metres.

$$\boxed{V_{tank} = \dfrac{P \cdot q \cdot n}{1000} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{]}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive, KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU Module 4 type):** A flood has displaced **4000 people** into a relief camp. The camp has **50 cattle** and **one 30-bed temporary hospital**. Water is supplied by tankers of capacity **10,000 L**, each making **2 trips/day**. Adopting Sphere Standards and including a peak factor of **1.5**, calculate:
> (a) Total daily water demand of the camp.
> (b) Number of tankers required per day.
> (c) Volume of storage tank needed for **2-day** autonomy.

### Part (a) — Total Daily Water Demand

**Population component (Sphere standard, 15 L/p/d):**

We first compute the water demand of the displaced population using the Sphere-recommended per-capita rate of 15 L per person per day for emergency response.

$$W_{pop} = 4000 \times 15 = 60{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

**Livestock component (cattle, 30 L/head/day):**

Next, we compute the water demand of the cattle, using the standard rate of 30 L per cattle per day as specified in the NDMA livestock-water guidelines.

$$W_{cattle} = 50 \times 30 = 1500 \text{ L/day}$$

**Hospital component (Sphere, 100 L/patient/day):**

The hospital water demand is calculated by multiplying the number of in-patient beds by the Sphere-standard 100 L per patient per day. The medical staff and OPD load are accounted for by the 100 L/bed rate.

$$W_{hospital} = 30 \times 100 = 3000 \text{ L/day}$$

**Total daily demand:**

The total water demand of the camp is the algebraic sum of the three demand categories: population, livestock, and hospital.

$$W_{total} = 60{,}000 + 1500 + 3000 = 64{,}500 \text{ L/day}$$

**Applying peak factor (1.5):**

The peak factor accounts for the morning concentration of demand and unforeseen losses. Multiplying by the peak factor gives the design (worst-case) demand that the supply system must handle.

$$W_{design} = 64{,}500 \times 1.5 = 96{,}750 \text{ L/day}$$

### Part (b) — Number of Tankers Required

**Per-tanker daily delivery:**

Each tanker makes 2 trips per day, carrying 10,000 L per trip. Therefore one tanker delivers 20,000 L per day.

$$W_{tanker} = 10{,}000 \times 2 = 20{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

**Number of tankers:**

The number of tankers required is the design daily demand divided by the daily delivery capacity of one tanker. The result is rounded up to the next whole number because a partial tanker cannot be deployed.

$$N = \dfrac{96{,}750}{20{,}000} = 4.84$$

$$\boxed{N = 5 \text{ tankers (rounded up)}}$$

### Part (c) — Storage Tank Capacity (2-Day Autonomy)

The storage tank must hold 2 days of design demand. We multiply the design daily demand by the number of days of autonomy and convert litres to cubic metres by dividing by 1000.

$$V_{tank} = \dfrac{96{,}750 \times 2}{1000} = \dfrac{193{,}500}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{V_{tank} = 193.5 \text{ m}^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Verification Trick:** A quick sanity check — for 4000 people, a 15 L/p/d demand gives 60,000 L/day, which is exactly **60 m³/day**. A 2-day tank of ~120 m³ is the *non-peak* figure; the peak-factor design of ~193.5 m³ is the *engineering-conservative* figure. The difference (~60% more) is what you would show in a design report to defend the larger tank.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Per-Capita Value

| Scenario | Phase | Recommended $q$ (L/p/d) | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Earthquake, first 24 h, no infrastructure | Emergency | 3 | Survival only — bottled/tanker |
| Flood, day 2–7, partial pipelines | Stabilization | 7.5 | Sphere *minimum* for survival hygiene |
| Cyclone shelter, day 7–30 | Stabilization | 15 | Sphere *standard* for health |
| Long-term displacement camp, >1 month | Recovery | 20–25 | NDMA / Govt. of India camp standard |
| Hospital in-patient (any phase) | All phases | 100 | Sphere *medical* standard |
| Cholera/diarrhoea outbreak in camp | Acute emergency | 25–50 (raised) | WHO outbreak response |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Water Demand Composition Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Camp Water Demand] --> B[Population Demand]
    A --> C[Livestock Demand]
    A --> D[Institutional Demand]
    A --> E[Peak Factor Multiplier]
    B --> B1[Drinking 3 Lpd]
    B --> B2[Cooking 2 to 3 Lpd]
    B --> B3[Personal Hygiene 4 to 6 Lpd]
    B --> B4[Laundry 2 to 4 Lpd]
    B --> B5[Shelter Cleaning 2 to 3 Lpd]
    C --> C1[Cattle 30 Lpd]
    C --> C2[Goats and Sheep 5 Lpd]
    C --> C3[Poultry Variable]
    D --> D1[Hospital 100 L per bed per day]
    D --> D2[Feeding Centre 15 L per beneficiary]
    D --> D3[Schools as Shelters 15 L per person]
    E --> E1[Morning Peak 1.5 to 2.0]
    E --> E2[Unforeseen Wastage]
    B1 --> F[Subtotal L per day]
    B2 --> F
    B3 --> F
    B4 --> F
    B5 --> F
    F --> G[Final Design Demand]
    C1 --> G
    C2 --> G
    C3 --> G
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    G --> H[Supply via Tankers and Storage Tank]
```

## 4.2 Disaster Phase vs. Water Supply Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Emergency 0 to 72 hours] --> P2[Phase 2 Stabilization 3 days to 1 month]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Recovery 1 to 6 months]
    P1 --> S1[Source Bottled Water and Tankers]
    P2 --> S2[Source Tankers plus Temporary Piped Network]
    P3 --> S3[Source Restored Municipal plus Augmented Supply]
    P1 --> Q1[Quantity 3 to 7.5 L per person per day]
    P2 --> Q2[Quantity 15 L per person per day]
    P3 --> Q3[Quantity 20 to 25 L per person per day]
    P1 --> T1[Treatment Chlorination plus Bladder Tanks]
    P2 --> T2[Treatment Filtration plus Chlorination]
    P3 --> T3[Treatment Conventional Treatment Restored]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Emergency Water Supply System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Water Source River Well Borewell Tanker] --> RAW[Raw Water Quality Test]
    RAW --> TREAT[Treatment Unit Chlorination plus Filtration]
    TREAT --> STOR[Storage Tank 2 day Autonomy]
    STOR --> PUMP[Distribution Pump or Gravity Main]
    PUMP --> DIST[Distribution Network]
    DIST --> TAPS[Communal Taps Max 250 persons per tap]
    DIST --> HOSP[Hospital Line Priority]
    DIST --> LIVSTK[Livestock Troughs]
    PUMP --> MON[Quality Monitoring at Taps]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Reading Tip:** The block diagram above is a common KTU exam question: *"Draw the block diagram of an emergency water supply system."* Memorize the **Source → Treatment → Storage → Distribution → End-user** chain and the **250 persons/tap** standard.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the term "Water Needs" in the context of disaster management. State the Sphere Standard minimum per-capita water requirement for emergency situations.**

**Model Answer:**

Water Needs in disaster management refers to the **quantitative and qualitative requirement of water** for sustaining human life, hygiene, livestock, and institutional functions during the emergency, stabilization, and recovery phases of a disaster.

The **Sphere Standard (2018)** specifies:
- **Minimum survival standard: 3 L per person per day** (drinking only).
- **Average minimum for health protection: 15 L per person per day** (drinking + cooking + personal hygiene + basic cleaning).

The 15 L/p/d standard is the most commonly adopted benchmark in KTU answers because it represents the *health-maintaining threshold*, not the survival threshold.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**List any six components of water needs in a relief camp and state the typical per-capita value for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Typical Value (L/p/d) |
|---|---|
| Drinking water | 3 |
| Cooking | 2–3 |
| Personal hygiene | 4–6 |
| Laundry | 2–4 |
| Shelter/toilet cleaning | 2–3 |
| Institutional (hospitals, schools-as-shelters) | 15–100 |

(Total typical sum: 15–25 L/p/d for a relief camp.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Sphere Standards** for water supply in disaster response, with reference to the different phases of disaster. State the per-capita standards and the rationale behind them.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A flood-affected village has **2500 displaced people, 200 cattle, and a 20-bed medical camp**. Water is being supplied through tankers of capacity **8000 L**, each making **3 trips per day**. Calculate the **total daily water demand** (Sphere Standards) and the **number of tankers required**. Use a peak factor of **1.5**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**The Sphere Standards for Water Supply:**

The Sphere Handbook, published by the Sphere Association (current edition: 2018), sets globally recognized minimum standards for humanitarian response. For water supply, the key standard is the **per-capita quantity of water** that must be available to disaster-affected populations to prevent mortality and morbidity.

**Per-Capita Standards (by phase):**

- **Emergency phase (0–72 hours):** A bare minimum of **3 L per person per day** is the *survival threshold*. This covers drinking only. It is set by WHO on the basis of the minimum fluid intake required to prevent dehydration in a temperate climate.

- **Stabilization phase (3 days–1 month):** The **average minimum standard is 15 L per person per day**. This is the *health-maintaining threshold*, sufficient to cover drinking, cooking, personal hygiene, and basic cleaning. Below 15 L, the incidence of water-borne and water-washed diseases (cholera, typhoid, trachoma, scabies) rises sharply.

- **Recovery phase (1–6 months):** The aim is to reach **20–25 L per person per day**, comparable to normal domestic consumption.

**Rationale for the Standards:**

The 15 L threshold was derived from epidemiological studies showing that water-borne disease outbreaks in refugee camps consistently correlate with per-capita supplies below 15 L. The 3 L survival threshold is derived from physiological studies of human water balance.

**Other Sphere Requirements:**
- Maximum **250 persons per tap** (to avoid queuing >30 minutes).
- Maximum **500 m distance** from dwelling to nearest water point.
- Water source within **30 minutes round-trip walk**.
- Water quality: **0 faecal coliforms per 100 mL** at point of delivery; **free chlorine residual of 0.5 mg/L**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Population water demand (Sphere 15 L/p/d):**

The displaced population's daily water demand is calculated by multiplying the number of people by the Sphere-standard per-capita rate of 15 L per person per day.

$$W_{pop} = 2500 \times 15 = 37{,}500 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 2 — Livestock water demand (cattle @ 30 L/head/day):**

The cattle water demand is found by multiplying the number of cattle by the standard per-animal rate of 30 L per day, as per NDMA livestock guidelines.

$$W_{cattle} = 200 \times 30 = 6000 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 3 — Hospital water demand (Sphere 100 L/bed/day):**

The hospital water demand is the number of in-patient beds multiplied by the Sphere medical standard of 100 L per patient per day, which includes the needs of medical staff and basic surgical support.

$$W_{hospital} = 20 \times 100 = 2000 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 4 — Total daily demand (pre-peak):**

The total pre-peak daily demand is the algebraic sum of the three demand categories: population, livestock, and hospital.

$$W_{total} = 37{,}500 + 6000 + 2000 = 45{,}500 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 5 — Design demand with peak factor (1.5):**

The peak factor of 1.5 is applied to the pre-peak demand to account for the morning peak in consumption and unforeseen losses such as leakage and wastage. The result is the design demand that the supply system must meet.

$$W_{design} = 45{,}500 \times 1.5 = 68{,}250 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 6 — Per-tanker daily delivery:**

Each tanker makes 3 trips per day carrying 8000 L per trip, so the daily delivery per tanker is the product of capacity and trips.

$$W_{tanker} = 8000 \times 3 = 24{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 7 — Number of tankers required:**

The number of tankers needed is the design daily demand divided by the per-tanker daily delivery, rounded up to the next whole number since a partial tanker cannot be deployed in practice.

$$N = \dfrac{68{,}250}{24{,}000} = 2.84$$

$$\boxed{N = 3 \text{ tankers per day}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Alternative]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **sources of water** typically used in disaster situations, with their advantages and limitations. Explain why **water quality monitoring** is critical in the first 30 days of relief operations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A relief camp of **5000 people** must be provided with a **3-day water-storage tank**. Adopting **15 L/p/d** as the per-capita standard and applying a **peak factor of 1.8**, calculate the **tank volume in m³**. If the water is to be transported by tankers of **12,000 L capacity** making **2 trips/day**, how many tankers are needed **per day** to keep the tank full?

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Sources of Water in Disasters:**

1. **Surface sources — rivers, lakes, ponds:**
   - *Advantages:* Easily accessible, large volumes available, no pumping cost if gravity-fed.
   - *Limitations:* High contamination risk (turbidity, bacteria, chemical spills), requires full treatment chain.

2. **Groundwater sources — hand-pumps, borewells, dug wells:**
   - *Advantages:* Generally better microbial quality, protected from surface contamination.
   - *Limitations:* Slower to deploy, may be damaged in earthquakes, can run dry in droughts, may have chemical contamination (arsenic, fluoride).

3. **Tanker-trucked water:**
   - *Advantages:* Rapidly deployable, suitable for the first 72 hours.
   - *Limitations:* Expensive, supply-chain dependent, can run out.

4. **Bottled water and sachet water:**
   - *Advantages:* Pre-treated, immediate safe supply for the first 24–48 hours.
   - *Limitations:* Expensive at scale, generates plastic waste, logistically heavy.

5. **Rainwater harvesting (in cyclone/typhoon aftermath):**
   - *Advantages:* Free, abundant after cyclonic rains.
   - *Limitations:* Unreliable, requires storage and treatment.

6. **Municipal pipeline (if partially functional):**
   - *Advantages:* Pre-existing distribution network.
   - *Limitations:* Often damaged; pressure may be low; contamination at pipe breaks is common.

**Why Water Quality Monitoring is Critical in the First 30 Days:**

The first 30 days of a relief operation are the **highest-risk period for water-borne disease outbreaks**. Reasons include:

- **Displaced populations are concentrated** in relief camps, increasing fecal-oral transmission.
- **Sanitation infrastructure is often damaged**, leading to contamination of nearby surface water.
- **New water sources** may be hastily commissioned without proper quality checks.
- **Population immunity is often lowered** by malnutrition, stress, and co-morbidities.

**Critical monitoring parameters (WHO/EPA emergency standards):**
- **0 faecal coliforms per 100 mL** at point of delivery.
- **Free chlorine residual of 0.5 mg/L** (for chlorinated supplies).
- **Turbidity < 5 NTU** (for effective chlorination).
- **pH 6.5–8.5.**
- Absence of chemical contaminants (heavy metals, pesticides, hydrocarbons).

**Monitoring frequency:** Daily for the first 30 days, reducing to weekly thereafter as the system stabilizes. Failure to monitor has historically led to **cholera outbreaks in refugee camps** (e.g., Goma 1994 — over 50,000 deaths, directly linked to insufficient water quantity and quality).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Daily demand of the camp:**

The base daily demand is the population multiplied by the Sphere-standard per-capita rate of 15 L per person per day.

$$W_{base} = 5000 \times 15 = 75{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 2 — Design demand with peak factor (1.8):**

The design daily demand is the base demand multiplied by the peak factor of 1.8 to handle morning peaks and unforeseen losses. This is the demand the supply infrastructure must meet.

$$W_{design} = 75{,}000 \times 1.8 = 135{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 3 — Storage tank volume (3-day autonomy):**

The storage tank must hold 3 days of design demand to provide autonomy against supply disruption. The volume in litres is converted to cubic metres by dividing by 1000.

$$V_{tank} = \dfrac{135{,}000 \times 3}{1000} = \dfrac{405{,}000}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{V_{tank} = 405 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 4 — Per-tanker daily delivery:**

Each tanker makes 2 trips per day, carrying 12,000 L per trip. The daily delivery per tanker is the product of capacity and trips.

$$W_{tanker} = 12{,}000 \times 2 = 24{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

**Step 5 — Number of tankers per day to keep the tank full:**

The number of tankers required to refill the tank daily (replacing 1 day of consumption) is the design daily demand divided by the per-tanker daily delivery. The result is rounded up because a partial tanker cannot be deployed.

$$N = \dfrac{135{,}000}{24{,}000} = 5.625$$

$$\boxed{N = 6 \text{ tankers per day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark-Deductions):**
> 1. **Confusing the 3 L and 15 L values** — many students write 3 L for total camp demand, losing 2–3 marks. Always read the question: if it says "Sphere Standard" or "health-maintaining", it means **15 L/p/d**, not 3 L.
> 2. **Forgetting livestock and hospital demand** — in numerical problems, the question will list all categories. Marks are awarded for each category separately.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert litres to m³** in tank-volume questions (divide by 1000). The unit mismatch is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Not rounding up** the tanker count — a fractional tanker cannot exist. Round **up** to the next whole number.
> 5. **Omitting the peak factor** when the question states one explicitly — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Water Needs):**

- **Definition:** Water Needs = total water required by a disaster-affected population to sustain life, health, and basic functions.
- **Sphere Standards (memorize verbatim):**
  - **3 L/p/d** → survival (drinking only).
  - **15 L/p/d** → average minimum (drinking + cooking + hygiene + cleaning).
  - **20–25 L/p/d** → long-term camp standard.
  - **100 L/bed/day** → hospital.
  - **30 L/head/day** → cattle.
- **Phases of water demand:**
  - Phase 1 (0–72 h): bottled + tankers, 3–7.5 L/p/d.
  - Phase 2 (3 d–1 m): tankers + temp. pipelines, 15 L/p/d.
  - Phase 3 (1–6 m): restored municipal + augmented, 20–25 L/p/d.
- **Key formulas:**
  - $W = P \cdot q$
  - $V_{tank} = \dfrac{P \cdot q \cdot n}{1000}$ m³
  - $N_{tanker} = \dfrac{P \cdot q \cdot f_p}{C \cdot f}$
- **Demand components (always list all 7):** drinking, cooking, personal hygiene, laundry, shelter cleaning, institutional (hospital/feeding/school), livestock.
- **Source hierarchy (best to worst):** groundwater > treated surface > tanker > bottled. Surface water is *always* the riskiest.
- **Quality standards (WHO/Sphere):** 0 faecal coliforms/100 mL; free chlorine ≥ 0.5 mg/L; turbidity < 5 NTU.
- **Distribution standards:** ≤ 250 persons per tap; ≤ 500 m walk to nearest tap; ≤ 30 min round-trip.
- **Unit conversion traps:** 1 m³ = 1000 L; 1 Imperial gallon = 4.546 L; always check the question's units.
- **Peak factor:** 1.5–2.0; apply whenever the question mentions "peak" or "worst case".
- **Common KTU numerical:** (Population + livestock + hospital) × peak factor = design demand → tankers & tank volume.
- **Rule of thumb:** A 15 L/p/d camp needs roughly **1 m³ of water per 67 people per day**.
- **Disaster-specific concerns:** floods → contamination; droughts → source drying; earthquakes → pipeline breaks; tsunamis → saltwater intrusion.
- **Always list** the chain: Source → Quality Test → Treatment → Storage → Distribution → End-user.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sanitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET524 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sanitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Disaster Management**, **Sanitation** refers to the public health practice and engineering discipline concerned with the provision of safe excreta disposal, wastewater management, solid waste management, vector control, and the promotion of hygienic living conditions for disaster-affected populations. As per the **Sphere Standards (2018 Edition)** and the **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP) of India**, sanitation in emergencies is defined as:

> *"The safe collection, containment, transportation, treatment, and disposal of human excreta, household wastewater, and solid waste under conditions where normal infrastructure has been compromised, disrupted, or destroyed, with the primary objective of preventing the transmission of faecal-oral diseases and protecting public health."*

The **Sustainable Development Goal 6 (SDG 6)** explicitly targets access to adequate and equitable sanitation and hygiene for all, with **Indicator 6.2.1(b)** mandating that populations in disaster situations must have access to safely managed sanitation facilities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Invisible Enemy" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a city after a major flood. The visible damage is heartbreaking — collapsed buildings, stranded vehicles, and muddy streets. However, the **true silent killer** lurking in the aftermath is the lack of sanitation. When toilets stop working, sewage mixes with drinking water, and rotting waste attracts disease-carrying insects, an outbreak of **cholera, typhoid, or dysentery** can kill more people than the disaster itself. Sanitation is the **"invisible shield"** that separates survival from secondary disaster.
>
> **Real-World Historical Lesson:** The 2010 Haiti earthquake killed an estimated 220,000 people directly, but a subsequent **cholera outbreak** (caused by unsanitary conditions at a UN camp) killed nearly **10,000 more**. This is why the WHO and Sphere Handbook treat sanitation as a *life-saving intervention* on par with food and shelter.

## 1.3 Key Public Health Metrics (Sphere Standard Indicators)

The following internationally benchmarked indicators are critical for KTU examinations:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Sanitation Standards (Sphere Handbook 2018)**
> - **Maximum Toilet User Ratio:** **20 persons per toilet** (in the acute phase of a disaster).
> - **Minimum Toilet Separation:** Toilets must be located **no more than 50 metres** from dwellings and **no less than 30 metres** from any water source.
> - **Pit Latrine Depth:** Minimum **2 metres above the water table** to prevent groundwater contamination.
> - **Defecation Field Area:** Approximately **0.25 m² to 0.5 m² per person per day** for trench latrines in the emergency phase.
> - **Solid Waste Generation in Camps:** Approximately **0.5 kg per person per day**.

## 1.4 Disease Triad: The Faecal-Oral Transmission Chain

Sanitation failures trigger a cascade of water-borne, water-washed, water-based, and vector-borne diseases. KTU examiners frequently test this linkage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Sanitation-Related Diseases (Bradley Classification)**
> 1. **Water-borne diseases:** Cholera, Typhoid, Hepatitis A & E, Polio.
> 2. **Water-washed diseases:** Trachoma, Scabies, Skin infections, Diarrhoeal episodes.
> 3. **Water-based diseases:** Schistosomiasis, Guinea worm (Dracunculiasis).
> 4. **Water-related insect-vector diseases:** Malaria, Dengue, Filariasis.
> 5. **Diseases with sanitation as a primary barrier:** Ascaris (roundworm), Hookworm, Taenia (tapeworm), and Amoebiasis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sanitation Engineering Hierarchy in Disaster Response

Effective emergency sanitation is delivered through a **hierarchical framework** recognized globally by humanitarian agencies (UNHCR, IFRC, Oxfam, WHO).

### 2.1.1 The Sanitation System Decision Tree (4 Pillars)

**Pillar 1: Containment**
- The first engineering objective is to **contain** human excreta at the point of generation.
- Methods: Communal trench latrines, pit latrines, container-based toilets (e.g., the **"Peepoo bag"** and the Oxfam **"Garder"** squatting plate).

**Pillar 2: Emptying, Transport & Treatment**
- When pits are 75% full, sludge must be emptied using **manual or mechanized gulpers** (e.g., the **Vacutug**).
- Sludge is transported to stabilization ponds or buried in **fecal sludge treatment plants (FSTPs)**.

**Pillar 3: Disposal or Reuse**
- Treated sludge can be safely buried, incinerated, or composted for non-food agricultural use.

**Pillar 4: Monitoring & Behaviour Change**
- Continuous community-led total sanitation (CLTS) drives hygiene promotion.

### 2.1.2 Toilet Technology Selection Matrix (Engineer's Choice)

| **Technology** | **User Capacity (Persons)** | **Soil Suitability** | **Water Table Depth** | **Cost Index** | **Lifespan** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Simple Trench Latrine | 100–200 | Loose/Loamy | > 2 m | Very Low ($\downarrow$) | Days to Weeks |
| Pit Latrine (Shallow) | 50–100 | Alluvial | > 1.8 m | Low | 1–3 Months |
| Bore Hole Latrine | 20–50 | Rocky/Hard | > 5 m | Medium | 3–6 Months |
| VIP Latrine (Ventilated Improved Pit) | 20–50 | Stable Soil | > 2 m | Medium | 1–2 Years |
| Septic Tank System | 50–500 | Stable Soil | > 1.5 m | High ($\uparrow$) | 5–10 Years |
| Container-Based Toilet | 20–50 | Any Terrain | No Limit | High (Recurring) | Indefinite |
| Portable Chemical Toilet | 10–30 | Any Terrain | No Limit | High (Recurring) | Indefinite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"Why are VIP latrines preferred over simple pit latrines in long-term camps?"*
> **Answer:** VIP latrines incorporate a **ventilation pipe (110 mm diameter)** and a **fly screen**, which prevents flies from transmitting pathogens from the pit to the users. The Venturi effect of the wind draws odours and flies upward, reducing disease transmission by approximately **90%**.

## 2.2 Solid Waste Management in Disasters

Solid waste in disaster settings includes debris, medical waste, food waste, and corpses. The management follows the **"5 R" Hierarchy** sanctioned by the **Solid Waste Management Rules (SWM), 2016 of India**.

### 2.2.1 The 5 R Hierarchy (Descending Priority)

1. **Refuse** – Do not accept unnecessary materials into the camp.
2. **Reduce** – Minimize packaging of relief materials.
3. **Reuse** – Re-purpose materials (e.g., flattened cans as roofing sheets).
4. **Recycle** – Sort organic and inorganic fractions.
5. **Recover** – Convert organic waste to compost or biogas.

### 2.2.2 Vector Control Calculations

Vector control targets disease-carrying organisms, primarily mosquitoes and flies. The **Breteau Index (BI)** is the standard entomological metric.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Equations for KTU 2024 Sanitation Problems**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Breteau Index (BI)} &= \frac{\text{Number of positive containers}}{\text{Number of houses inspected}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Container Index (CI)} &= \frac{\text{Number of positive containers}}{\text{Number of containers inspected}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{House Index (HI)} &= \frac{\text{Number of houses infested}}{\text{Total houses inspected}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pupae per Person Index (PPI)} &= \frac{\text{Number of pupae collected}}{\text{Total number of people in the area}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{trench}} &= L \times W \times D
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $L$ = Length of trench (metres)
- $W$ = Width of trench (typically 0.3 m, accommodating 1 user at a time)
- $D$ = Depth of trench (typically 1.0 m to 1.5 m)
- $V_{\text{trench}}$ = Volume of trench in cubic metres (m³)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{latrine life}} &= \frac{V_{\text{trench}}}{N_{\text{users}} \times R_{\text{sludge}} \times T_{\text{use}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{latrine life}}$ = Time until the latrine pit is full (days)
- $V_{\text{trench}}$ = Trench volume (m³)
- $N_{\text{users}}$ = Number of users (persons)
- $R_{\text{sludge}}$ = Sludge accumulation rate ($\approx 0.04$ m³ per person per year for pits)
- $T_{\text{use}}$ = Time conversion factor (365 days/year)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{water}} &= P_{\text{pop}} \times q_{\text{per capita}} \times F_{\text{peak}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{water}}$ = Required water flow (litres/day)
- $P_{\text{pop}}$ = Population in camp
- $q_{\text{per capita}}$ = Per capita water demand ($\approx 15$ L/day emergency minimum, **Sphere Standard**)
- $F_{\text{peak}}$ = Peak factor (typically 1.5 to 2.0)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above formulae are deployed in real disaster response by the **National Disaster Response Force (NDRF)**, **State Disaster Response Forces (SDRF)**, and international bodies like **UN-OCHA**. Engineers must size trenches before monsoon, calculate latrine lifespan to plan pit rotation, and compute water demand to size overhead tanks and pipeline diameters. The Breteau Index guides **Indoor Residual Spraying (IRS)** cycles to prevent malaria outbreaks in the post-disaster phase.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Sizing an Emergency Trench Latrine

**Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU 2024 Pattern):**
A relief camp is sheltering **400 displaced persons** following a major flood. The sanitary engineer must design a simple trench latrine with the following specifications:
- Width of trench, $W = 0.3$ m
- Depth of trench, $D = 1.5$ m
- Sludge accumulation rate, $R_{\text{sludge}} = 0.04$ m³/person/year
- Required lifespan of trench before backfilling, $T_{\text{use}} = 90$ days (acute phase)

**Step 1: Convert the sludge accumulation rate to a daily rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{sludge daily}} &= \frac{0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{person/year}}{365 \text{ days/year}} \\
&= 1.096 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{person/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

This represents the volume of excreta + cover material (soil) generated per person per day inside the pit.

**Step 2: Calculate the total daily sludge volume for the entire population.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{daily total}} &= N_{\text{users}} \times R_{\text{sludge daily}} \\
&= 400 \times 1.096 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 0.04384 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the total sludge volume over the desired 90-day lifespan.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= V_{\text{daily total}} \times 90 \\
&= 0.04384 \times 90 \\
&= 3.9456 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Determine the required length $L$ of the trench.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{trench}} &= L \times W \times D \\
L &= \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{W \times D} \\
L &= \frac{3.9456}{0.3 \times 1.5} \\
L &= \frac{3.9456}{0.45} \\
L &= 8.768 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Sphere Standard user ratio for design refinement.**

According to Sphere Standards, the maximum ratio is **20 persons per toilet seat**. For 400 persons:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{toilets}} &= \frac{400 \text{ persons}}{20 \text{ persons/toilet}} = 20 \text{ toilet seats}
\end{aligned}
$$

If each toilet seat occupies **0.6 m of trench length** (allowing privacy partitions):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{required}} &= 20 \times 0.6 = 12.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Adopt the maximum value for safety.**

$$
L_{\text{designed}} = \max(8.768 \text{ m}, 12.0 \text{ m}) = 12.0 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Engineering Answer:** The trench should be **12.0 metres long, 0.3 m wide, and 1.5 m deep**, partitioned into 20 cubicles of 0.6 m each, accommodating 400 persons for 90 days.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Breteau Index Risk Classification

**Problem Statement:**
A public health team inspects **200 houses** in a post-cyclone camp. They find that **40 houses** contain water-holding containers, and out of **600 total containers** inspected, **90 containers** are positive for *Aedes* mosquito larvae.

**Step 1: Calculate the Breteau Index.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BI} &= \frac{\text{Number of positive containers}}{\text{Number of houses inspected}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{90}{200} \times 100 \\
&= 45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Container Index.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CI} &= \frac{\text{Number of positive containers}}{\text{Number of containers inspected}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{90}{600} \times 100 \\
&= 15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the House Index.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HI} &= \frac{\text{Number of houses infested}}{\text{Total houses inspected}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{40}{200} \times 100 \\
&= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Risk Classification using WHO Thresholds (for *Aedes aegypti*).**

| **Breteau Index Value** | **Risk Level** | **Recommended Action** |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $\text{BI} < 5$ | Low Risk | Routine surveillance. |
| $5 \leq \text{BI} < 20$ | Medium Risk | Larviciding, source reduction, community mobilization. |
| $\text{BI} \geq 20$ | High Risk | Emergency vector control, IRS, ULV fogging, health department alert. |

Since our computed **BI = 45**, the area is classified as **HIGH RISK** for Dengue/Chikungunya transmission. Immediate vector control operations must be launched.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse the **Breteau Index** with the **Container Index**. Remember: **BI uses houses in the denominator; CI uses containers in the denominator.** This is the single most common error in KTU sanitation numerical problems.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Camp Water Demand Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A relief camp houses **1,000 displaced persons**. The per capita emergency water demand is **15 L/day**, and the peak factor is **1.8** (morning and evening peak usage).

**Step 1: Calculate the average daily water demand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{average}} &= 1000 \times 15 = 15{,}000 \text{ L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the peak factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{peak}} &= 15{,}000 \times 1.8 = 27{,}000 \text{ L/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to litres per minute for pump sizing.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{design}} &= \frac{27{,}000}{24 \times 60} = 18.75 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

The engineer should specify a pump with a capacity of at least **20 L/min** at the required total dynamic head (TDH).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Emergency Sanitation Response Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the end-to-end functional flow of emergency sanitation operations, from initial rapid assessment through to long-term community handover.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STAGE 1: RAPID NEEDS ASSESSMENT"] --> nodeB{"Is existing sanitation infrastructure functional?"}
    nodeB -- "NO" --> nodeC["STAGE 2: EMERGENCY PHASE 0 to 14 DAYS"]
    nodeB -- "YES - DAMAGED" --> nodeD["STAGE 3: TRANSITIONAL PHASE 15 to 60 DAYS"]
    nodeB -- "YES - INTACT" --> nodeE["STAGE 4: STABILIZATION PHASE 60 PLUS DAYS"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["DEPLOY TRENCH LATRINES AND COMMUNAL PITS"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["SET UP DEFECATION FIELDS WITH BURYING"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["DISTRIBUTE HYGIENE KITS AND WATER PURIFICATION"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["USER RATIO TARGET 1 TO 50 PERSONS PER TOILET"]
    nodeD --> nodeJ["CONSTRUCT VIP LATRINES AND SEPTIC TANKS"]
    nodeD --> nodeK["INSTALL HANDWASHING STATIONS WITH SOAP"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["USER RATIO TARGET 1 TO 20 PERSONS PER TOILET"]
    nodeE --> nodeM["REHABILITATE EXISTING SEWAGE NETWORKS"]
    nodeE --> nodeN["BUILD DECENTRALIZED WASTEWATER TREATMENT SYSTEMS"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["USER RATIO TARGET 1 TO 15 PERSONS PER TOILET"]
    nodeI --> nodeP["CONTINUOUS VECTOR CONTROL SPRAYING"]
    nodeK --> nodeP
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeH --> nodeQ["BEHAVIOUR CHANGE COMMUNICATION DRIVE"]
    nodeL --> nodeQ
    nodeO --> nodeQ
    nodeP --> nodeR["MONITORING AND DISEASE SURVEILLANCE"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS{"Any disease outbreak detected?"}
    nodeS -- "YES" --> nodeT["EMERGENCY HEALTH RESPONSE ACTIVATED"]
    nodeS -- "NO" --> nodeU["HANDOVER TO LOCAL MUNICIPAL AUTHORITY"]
    nodeT --> nodeR
```

## 4.2 Faecal-Oral Transmission Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["HUMAN EXCRETA IN OPEN ENVIRONMENT"] --> node2["PATHWAY 1: WATER CONTAMINATION"]
    node1 --> node3["PATHWAY 2: SOIL CONTAMINATION"]
    node1 --> node4["PATHWAY 3: FLY TRANSPORT"]
    node1 --> node5["PATHWAY 4: FINGER CONTACT"]
    node1 --> node6["PATHWAY 5: FOOD CONTAMINATION"]
    node2 --> node7["INFECTED PERSON SUFFERS CHOLERA OR TYPHOID"]
    node3 --> node7
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8["SECONDARY COMMUNITY TRANSMISSION"]
    node8 --> node9["OUTBREAK DECLARATION BY DISTRICT HEALTH OFFICER"]
    node9 --> node10["SANITATION BARRIER INTERVENTION TARGETING NODE1"]
    node10 --> node1
```

## 4.3 Toilet Technology Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: ASSESS TERRAIN AND WATER TABLE"] --> nodeB{"Is water table > 2 metres deep?"}
    nodeB -- "YES" --> nodeC{"Is duration < 1 month?"}
    nodeB -- "NO" --> nodeD["USE CONTAINER BASED OR CHEMICAL TOILET"]
    nodeC -- "YES" --> nodeE["DEPLOY SIMPLE TRENCH LATRINE"]
    nodeC -- "NO" --> nodeF{"Is soil stable for digging?"}
    nodeF -- "YES" --> nodeG["DEPLOY VIP LATRINE OR POUR FLUSH TOILET"]
    nodeF -- "NO" --> nodeH["DEPLOY RAISED PLATFORM LATRINE"]
    nodeG --> nodeI{"Is population > 5000 persons?"}
    nodeI -- "YES" --> nodeJ["DESIGN SEPTIC TANK WITH DRAIN FIELD"]
    nodeI -- "NO" --> nodeK["USE VIP LATRINE WITH REGULAR SLUDGE EMPTYING"]
    nodeD --> nodeL["PLAN WEEKLY COLLECTION AND TREATMENT"]
    nodeE --> nodeM["END: MONTHLY MONITORING"]
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Sanitation" as applicable to disaster management. State any two Sphere Standards related to emergency sanitation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Sanitation in disaster management refers to the safe collection, containment, transportation, treatment, and disposal of human excreta, wastewater, and solid waste, along with the promotion of hygienic practices, to prevent the outbreak of faecal-oral diseases in populations affected by disasters.

The two key Sphere Standards are:

1. **Maximum Toilet User Ratio:** A maximum of **20 persons per toilet** in the acute emergency phase, reducing to **15 persons per toilet** in the stabilization phase.
2. **Minimum Toilet Distance:** Toilets must be located no more than **50 metres** from dwellings, with separate facilities for men, women, and children, and a minimum **30-metre separation** from any drinking water source.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for first standard, 1 mark for second standard.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any four water-borne diseases commonly seen in the aftermath of a flood disaster, and explain how improper sanitation contributes to their spread.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four water-borne diseases are:
1. **Cholera** (caused by *Vibrio cholerae*)
2. **Typhoid** (caused by *Salmonella typhi*)
3. **Hepatitis A and E** (caused by Hepatitis viruses)
4. **Acute Diarrhoeal Disease (ADD)**

**Mechanism of spread via poor sanitation:** During floods, pit latrines, septic tanks, and sewage pipelines overflow and rupture, releasing faecal matter into stagnant water bodies. When this contaminated water is consumed without boiling or chlorination, or when it comes into contact with food prepared in the camp, the pathogens enter the human digestive system, leading to outbreaks.

*(Award 1.5 marks for listing the diseases, 1.5 marks for the mechanism explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the engineering design of a Ventilated Improved Pit (VIP) Latrine. Explain why it is considered superior to a simple pit latrine in long-term relief camps.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Design Components of a VIP Latrine:**

1. **Pit (Lowering Section):** A circular or rectangular pit of diameter **1.2 m to 1.5 m**, dug to a depth of **3 m to 4 m**, ensuring at least **2 m of soil above the water table**.

2. **Slab and Squat Plate:** A reinforced concrete slab (75 mm thick) or a sturdy wooden slab fitted with a **squatting plate** and a **drop hole** of size 250 mm × 350 mm.

3. **Ventilation Pipe:** A vertical PVC or asbestos-cement pipe of **110 mm internal diameter**, extending from the pit upward to a height of at least **0.5 m above the roof** of the superstructure. The pipe is painted **black on the outside** to absorb solar heat, creating a thermal updraft.

4. **Fly Screen:** A **nylon or stainless-steel fly screen** with **1.2 mm to 1.5 mm aperture** is fitted at the top of the ventilation pipe. This screen prevents flies from escaping but allows air to flow out.

5. **Superstructure:** A simple shelter made of bamboo, CGI sheets, or thatch, providing privacy and protection from rain. It is kept dark inside to prevent flies from being attracted downward.

6. **Outlet Vent:** A separate vent is provided on the outside wall of the superstructure to allow fresh air circulation, supporting the Venturi effect.

**Why VIP is Superior to Simple Pit Latrine (4 Advantages):**

1. **Fly Control Mechanism:** The thermal updraft in the ventilation pipe draws air from the pit upward and out. Flies inside the pit are attracted upward by the light at the top, get trapped at the fly screen, and die of dehydration. This **breaks the faecal-fly-food-finger transmission cycle**.

2. **O dour Mitigation:** Continuous updraft of air removes foul gases (methane, hydrogen sulphide) from the pit, making the latrine more acceptable to users and reducing open defecation.

3. **Longer Lifespan:** Due to a deeper pit and better design, a VIP latrine lasts **1.5 to 2 years**, compared to **1 to 3 months** for a trench latrine, making it suitable for transitional phase camps.

4. **Lower Water Table Sensitivity:** With proper site selection, VIP latrines can be used in areas with moderately high water tables (down to 1.5 m depth) by raising the pit walls above ground.

*(Award 3 marks for design, 4 marks for advantages explanation.)*

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** An emergency relief camp is sheltering 2,000 displaced persons. The camp sanitary engineer decides to construct trench latrines with the following specifications: trench width = 0.3 m, trench depth = 1.5 m, sludge accumulation rate = 0.04 m³/person/year, desired service life = 120 days. Calculate the total length of the trench required. Verify whether your design meets the Sphere Standard of 20 persons per toilet, assuming 0.6 m of trench length per toilet seat.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Convert sludge rate to daily basis.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{sludge daily}} &= \frac{0.04}{365} = 1.096 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{person/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Daily sludge volume for 2,000 persons.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{daily}} &= 2000 \times 1.096 \times 10^{-4} = 0.2192 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total sludge volume over 120 days.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= 0.2192 \times 120 = 26.3 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate trench length from sludge volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{sludge}} &= \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{W \times D} = \frac{26.3}{0.3 \times 1.5} = \frac{26.3}{0.45} = 58.45 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate trench length from Sphere Standard (1 toilet per 20 persons).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{toilets}} &= \frac{2000}{20} = 100 \text{ toilet seats} \\
L_{\text{standard}} &= 100 \times 0.6 = 60.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Adopt the greater of the two lengths for safe design.**

$$
L_{\text{designed}} = \max(58.45, 60.0) = 60.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion:** The trench must be **60 m long, 0.3 m wide, and 1.5 m deep**, accommodating 100 toilet seats and 2,000 persons for 120 days. The design satisfies the Sphere Standard.

*(Award 2 marks for sludge calculation, 2 marks for length calculation, 2 marks for Sphere verification, 1 mark for final conclusion.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** What is a Breteau Index? Explain its significance in post-disaster vector control. A post-flood camp survey inspects 150 houses and finds 60 positive containers out of 450 total containers inspected. Calculate the Breteau Index, Container Index, and House Index. Classify the risk level using WHO thresholds.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Definition of Breteau Index (2 Marks):**
The Breteau Index (BI) is an entomological surveillance metric used in public health engineering to estimate the density of mosquito larvae (especially *Aedes* species) in a defined geographic area. It is defined as the **number of positive containers per 100 houses inspected**.

**Significance in Post-Disaster Vector Control (2 Marks):**
- It provides a **quantitative measure of dengue, chikungunya, and Zika transmission risk** in relief camps where water storage is abundant.
- It helps decision-makers trigger **larviciding, source reduction, and ultra-low-volume (ULV) fogging** operations.
- The WHO uses a BI threshold of **5 (for *Aedes aegypti* in tropical regions)** to declare a high-risk zone, prompting emergency vector control measures.

**Calculations (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BI} &= \frac{60}{150} \times 100 = 40 \\
\text{CI} &= \frac{60}{450} \times 100 = 13.33 \\
\text{HI} &= \frac{\text{Assumed houses infested}}{150} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming the problem implies a 1:1 ratio between positive containers and infested houses (worst case):

$$
\text{HI} = \frac{60}{150} \times 100 = 40
$$

**Risk Classification:** With **BI = 40**, the camp is classified as **HIGH RISK** for dengue transmission (since BI $\geq 20$ according to WHO). Emergency vector control must be initiated.

*(Award 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for significance, 2 marks for calculation, 1 mark for risk classification.)*

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of hygiene promotion and behaviour change communication (BCC) in disaster sanitation. Mention at least four key messages that are typically communicated to disaster-affected communities.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Role of Hygiene Promotion and BCC in Disaster Sanitation (4 Marks):**

1. **Sustains Infrastructure Benefits:** Construction of toilets and water points is futile if people continue open defecation. BCC ensures the **actual utilization of facilities** by addressing cultural, religious, and habitual barriers.

2. **Prevents Secondary Disease Outbreaks:** Awareness of faecal-oral transmission routes encourages hand-washing at critical moments (before eating, after defecation), breaking the disease transmission chain.

3. **Empowers Community Ownership:** BCC transforms beneficiaries into active participants. Trained community hygiene promoters (typically 1 per 100–150 persons) become long-term custodians of sanitation infrastructure.

4. **Targets Vulnerable Groups:** BCC is tailored to reach women, children, the elderly, and the disabled, addressing their specific sanitation needs (e.g., menstrual hygiene management, child faeces disposal).

5. **Integrates with WASH Cluster:** In the UN Cluster System, hygiene promotion is one of the four pillars of the WASH (Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene) response, alongside water supply, excreta disposal, and solid waste management.

**Four Key Hygiene Messages (3 Marks):**

1. **"Wash your hands with soap at critical moments"** — before eating, before preparing food, after defecation, and after handling a child’s faeces.

2. **"Use the latrine. Do not defecate in the open."** — emphasizing the health risks of open defecation and the safe distance to maintain from water sources.

3. **"Boil or chlorinate all drinking water."** — explaining the importance of safe water and demonstrating household-level water treatment methods.

4. **"Safely dispose of child faeces and manage menstrual hygiene."** — addressing the often-overlooked sanitary needs of children and women.

*(Award 4 marks for role discussion, 3 marks for the four key messages.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Numerical Answer Rounding:** Always carry intermediate calculations to **at least 4 decimal places** before final rounding. Many students lose 1 mark by premature rounding.
> 2. **Sphere Standard Misquotation:** The student often writes "50 persons per toilet" — the correct ratio is **20 persons per toilet** in the acute phase.
> 3. **Confusing Index Formulas:** Breteau Index uses **houses in the denominator**, while Container Index uses **containers in the denominator**. Swap them and you lose the entire 7 marks in that sub-question.
> 4. **Ignoring Women-Friendly Toilet Design:** In Part B answers, students must mention **separate toilets for men and women, lockable doors, and menstrual hygiene management** to score full marks on BCC questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
