<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Information Administration in the context of disaster management. List its four main pillars. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Information Administration in disaster management is the systematic process of acquiring, organizing, processing, analyzing, storing, disseminating, and archiving disaster-relevant information across the pre-, during-, and post-disaster phases to support evidence-based decision making and effective response.

**Four Main Pillars:**
1. **Information Needs Assessment (INA)**
2. **Information Acquisition and Processing**
3. **Information Dissemination**
4. **Feedback and Iterative Improvement**

---

### Question 2: What is the Common Operating Picture (COP) and why is it critical in multi-agency disaster response? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Common Operating Picture (COP)** is a single, shared, continuously updated visual representation of the disaster situation that is accessible to all responding agencies (NDMA, NDRF, SDRF, Army, NGOs) in real time.

**Why it is critical:**
- Eliminates information silos between agencies
- Prevents duplication of effort
- Enables coordinated resource allocation
- Provides a shared situational awareness reducing response time
- Forms the operational basis for unified command under the **Incident Response System (IRS)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Information Management Cycle** in disaster management with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Sendai Framework in strengthening information administration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cyclone warning satellite uplink has $B = 50$ MHz bandwidth and $S/N = 30$ dB. Compute the Shannon channel capacity. If the signal power must be doubled to overcome heavy rain attenuation, what is the new capacity? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

The **Information Management Cycle (IMC)** consists of six interconnected stages:

1. **Identification of Information Needs:** Stakeholders (EOC, IMD, NDRF, district administration) jointly identify *what* information is required (e.g., cyclone track, storm surge, population in red zone).

2. **Information Acquisition:** Data is collected from:
   - Space-based platforms (INSAT, MODIS, Sentinel)
   - Ground sensors (Doppler Weather Radar, AWS)
   - Human sources (crowdsourcing via SMS, social media analytics)

3. **Information Processing and Analysis:** Raw data is cleaned, georeferenced, fused, and modeled (HWRF model for cyclone track, MIKE-11 for flood).

4. **Information Storage and Retrieval:** Stored in cloud GIS databases with metadata standards (ISO 19115) and redundant backups.

5. **Information Dissemination:** Multi-modal delivery via CAP protocol, sirens, SMS, radio, TV, and door-to-door volunteers. Must be multi-lingual and culturally appropriate.

6. **Feedback and Evaluation:** Post-event AAR, computation of POD, FAR, CSI, and update of SOPs.

**Role of the Sendai Framework (2015–2030):**
- **Priority 1:** Understanding disaster risk — emphasizes investment in hazard and vulnerability data.
- **Priority 3:** Investing in disaster risk reduction for resilience — promotes early warning multi-hazard systems.
- Calls for open data sharing between countries (e.g., cyclone data shared by IMD with Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Oman).
- Encourages use of traditional knowledge alongside satellite data.

**Block Diagram of IMC:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Needs Assessment] --> B[Acquisition]
    B --> C[Processing]
    C --> D[Storage]
    D --> E[Dissemination]
    E --> F[Feedback]
    F --> A
```

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1:** Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$

**Step 2:** Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem.

$$C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

$$C_1 = 50 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000)$$

$$C_1 = 50 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1001)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate logarithm.

$$\log_2(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 4:** Compute capacity.

$$C_1 = 50 \times 10^6 \times 9.967 = 4.9835 \times 10^8 \text{ bits/s}$$

$$\boxed{C_1 \approx 498 \text{ Mbps}}$$

**Step 5:** Double signal power → S/N doubles from 1000 to 2000.

$$\frac{S_{new}}{N} = 2 \times 1000 = 2000$$

$$C_2 = 50 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(2001)$$

$$\log_2(2001) = \frac{\log_{10}(2001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.3012}{0.3010} \approx 10.967$$

$$C_2 = 50 \times 10^6 \times 10.967 = 5.4837 \times 10^8 \text{ bits/s}$$

$$\boxed{C_2 \approx 548 \text{ Mbps} \;\; (\text{gain} \approx 50 \text{ Mbps})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget that **SNR in dB must be converted to linear scale** before applying Shannon's formula. Writing $\log_2(1+30)$ instead of $\log_2(1+1000)$ is a **3-mark deduction** in part (b). Always convert: $S/N_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(S/N_{\text{dB}}/10)}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **components of an Early Warning System (EWS)** and explain the **last-mile connectivity challenge** in rural India. How can the Common Alerting Protocol (CAP) address this? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automated landslide warning system recorded the following in a monsoon season: Hits = 35, False Alarms = 15, Misses = 5, Correct Negatives = 200. Calculate POD, FAR, and CSI. Comment on the reliability of the system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Components of an Early Warning System (EWS)** — based on UN-ISDR's four-element framework:

1. **Risk Knowledge:** Hazard maps, vulnerability assessments, historical data, demographic exposure.
2. **Monitoring and Warning Service:** Sensors, satellite feeds, forecasting models (Doppler radar, rainfall thresholds, soil moisture).
3. **Dissemination and Communication:** Multi-channel, multi-lingual, CAP-compliant infrastructure.
4. **Response Capability:** Trained community, evacuation plans, shelters, drills, and SOPs.

**Last-Mile Connectivity Challenge in Rural India:**
- Only ~45% rural household internet penetration (TRAI 2023).
- Mountainous terrain disrupts radio and cellular signals.
- Power outages common.
- Low literacy limits text-based warnings.
- Linguistic diversity (22 official languages, hundreds of dialects).
- Lack of trust in official sources among tribal/remote communities.

**How CAP addresses this:**
- **Common Alerting Protocol (CAP)** is an XML-based standard (ITU-T X.1303) that allows a single alert to be automatically translated and broadcast across **all channels simultaneously** — SMS, sirens, TV scroll, radio, mobile apps, social media, digital billboards.
- Supports multi-lingual payloads.
- Geo-targeted (polygon-based broadcast to specific villages/blocks).
- Includes urgency, severity, and instruction fields reducing ambiguity.
- Integrated with India's **Integrated Alert System (SACHET)** and **CWC flood warning system**.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Risk Knowledge] --> B[Monitoring]
    B --> C[Forecasting]
    C --> D[Dissemination via CAP]
    D --> E[Response]
    E -.Feedback.-> A
```

#### Model Solution to (b)

Given:
- $a = 35$, $b = 15$, $c = 5$, $d = 200$

**Step 1:** Calculate Probability of Detection (POD).

$$POD = \frac{a}{a+c} = \frac{35}{35+5} = \frac{35}{40} = 0.875$$

**Step 2:** Calculate False Alarm Ratio (FAR).

$$FAR = \frac{b}{a+b} = \frac{15}{35+15} = \frac{15}{50} = 0.30$$

**Step 3:** Calculate Critical Success Index (CSI).

$$CSI = \frac{a}{a+b+c} = \frac{35}{35+15+5} = \frac{35}{55} = 0.6364$$

**Step 4:** Calculate Bias Score.

$$B = \frac{a+b}{a+c} = \frac{35+15}{35+5} = \frac{50}{40} = 1.25$$

**Interpretation:**
- POD = 0.875 is **excellent** — system catches 87.5% of landslides.
- FAR = 0.30 is **acceptable** for landslide warning (less critical than cyclone/tornado).
- CSI = 0.636 indicates **good overall skill**.
- Bias = 1.25 shows **slight over-warning tendency**, which is desirable for landslides (false negatives are fatal).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In part (b), many students confuse **FAR with False Alarm Rate (F = b/(b+d))**. FAR uses *warnings* as denominator, not *non-events*. Writing $FAR = 15/(15+200) = 0.0698$ is **wrong** and attracts a **2-mark penalty**. Memorize: $FAR = b/(a+b)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Information Administration**

- ☐ **Definition:** Information Administration = acquire, process, store, analyze, disseminate, archive disaster info for evidence-based decisions.
- ☐ **Four Pillars:** INA → Acquisition → Dissemination → Feedback.
- ☐ **IMC:** 6-stage closed loop (Needs → Acquisition → Processing → Storage → Dissemination → Feedback).
- ☐ **Shannon Entropy:** $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ (bits) — measures information content of warning.
- ☐ **Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ (bps) — fundamental link budget.
- ☐ **Convert dB to linear:** $S/N_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(\text{dB}/10)}$ — most common error in exam.
- ☐ **EWS 4 elements (UN-ISDR):** Risk knowledge, Monitoring, Dissemination, Response capability.
- ☐ **CAP Protocol:** XML-based ITU-T X.1303 — multi-channel, multi-lingual, geo-targeted alerts.
- ☐ **POD formula:** $a/(a+c)$ — higher is better; target > 0.80.
- ☐ **FAR formula:** $b/(a+b)$ — lower is better; target < 0.30.
- ☐ **CSI formula:** $a/(a+b+c)$ — overall skill metric; > 0.60 considered good.
- ☐ **Lead Time for cyclone evacuation:** minimum 6 hours for mass evacuation success.
- ☐ **Parallel reliability:** $R_{sys} = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$ — used for redundant EOC communication channels.
- ☐ **Sendai Framework (2015–2030) Priority 1 & 3:** directly mandates improved information exchange and EWS.
- ☐ **Last-mile challenge in India:** rural internet gap (~55%), linguistic diversity, power unreliability.
- ☐ **Real-time success story:** Cyclone Biparjoy (2023) — IMD's 72-hour lead time enabled zero coastal casualties in Gujarat due to CAP-based multi-channel warning.
- ☐ **Real-time failure mode:** 2004 Tsunami — IMD's bulletin was generated but never reached coastal fishing villages (no last-mile system).
- ☐ **Common Operating Picture (COP):** shared GIS dashboard for all agencies — the operational goal of Information Administration.
- ☐ **Bias Score ideal = 1; > 1 means over-warning, < 1 means under-warning.**
- ☐ **Always quote ISO 19115** for metadata standards, **ITU-T X.1303** for CAP, and **Sendai Framework 2015–2030** as policy backbone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology in Disaster Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET524 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Technology in Disaster Management # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Technology in Disaster Management** refers to the systematic application of geospatial, informational, communicational, and analytical tools—including **Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**, **Global Positioning System (GPS)**, **Remote Sensing (RS)**, **Early Warning Systems (EWS)**, **Artificial Intelligence (AI)**, and **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)**—across the four phases of the disaster management cycle: **Mitigation, Preparedness, Response, and Recovery (MPRR)**. Under the **Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015–2030)**, technology acts as a *force multiplier* that enhances situational awareness, accelerates decision-making, and reduces the *time-to-information* during cascading hazard events. The KTU 2024 syllabus positions technology as the **operational backbone** that converts hazard data into actionable intelligence. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🧠 Think of it like a Hospital ICU for a City** > > Imagine a city falling sick during a flood or earthquake. Just as a doctor uses an **ECG machine** (senses), **MRI scanner** (analyzes), **defibrillator** (responds), and **monitoring screens** (communicates)—disaster managers use: > - **Sensors** → Satellites & Drones (Remote Sensing) > - **MRI-style analysis** → GIS mapping > - **Doctor's GPS** → Real-time GPS tracking of rescue teams > - **Defibrillator shock** → Early Warning Sirens & SMS alerts > - **ICU Monitor** → AI-based dashboards and decision-support systems > > *Without technology, disaster management is like treating a patient with bare hands in the dark.* --- ## 1.3 Core Technology Pillars (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight) | # | Technology Pillar | Core Function in DM | Standard Metric/Standard | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **GIS** | Spatial data layering, risk zoning | WGS-84 Datum, UTM Projection | | 2 | **GPS** | Real-time geo-tagging of assets & victims | Accuracy: **± 3 m** (civilian) | | 3 | **Remote Sensing** | Earth observation from space/airborne platforms | Spatial Res: **0.3 m – 1 km** | | 4 | **Early Warning Systems (EWS)** | Last-mile alert dissemination | Lead time: **6–72 hours** | | 5 | **UAVs / Drones** | Aerial reconnaissance, payload delivery | Flight time: **25–40 min** | | 6 | **AI / ML** | Predictive modelling, damage classification | Accuracy benchmark: **> 85%** | | 7 | **Communication Tech** | Resilient, multi-modal alert broadcasting | CAP-IPAWS Protocol | | 8 | **Social Media Analytics** | Crowdsourced situational awareness | Tweet rate: **6,000 tweets/sec** | --- ## 1.4 Definitions in Standard KTU Glossary Format > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Definitions for Board Exams:** > > 1. **GIS (Geographic Information System):** A computer-based tool that captures, stores, checks, integrates, manipulates, analyses and displays data with reference to positions on the Earth's surface (Burrough, 1986). > > 2. **Remote Sensing:** The acquisition of information about an object or phenomenon without making physical contact, typically via satellite or airborne sensor platforms. > > 3. **Early Warning System (EWS):** An integrated system of hazard monitoring, forecasting, risk assessment, communication, and preparedness activities that enables individuals, communities, and authorities to take timely action. > > 4. **Crowdsourcing:** The practice of obtaining needed services, ideas, or content by soliciting contributions from a large group of people, especially from online communities (Howe, 2006). > > 5. **UAV (Unmanned Aerial Vehicle):** An aircraft without a human pilot aboard, used for remote sensing, surveillance, and delivery in disaster zones. --- ## 1.5 Why Technology? — The "Why" Behind the Syllabus > [!NOTE] > **Three driving motivations from the Sendai Framework:** > 1. **Priority 1:** Understanding disaster risk (technology reveals *where* and *why*). > 2. **Priority 3:** Investing in disaster risk reduction for resilience (technology enables *foresight*). > 3. **Priority 4:** Enhancing disaster preparedness for effective response (technology enables *speed*). # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Disaster Technology Stack — Layered Architecture ### 🔹 Layer 1: Data Acquisition Layer (Sensors) This is the **"eyes and ears"** of disaster management. Data is gathered through: - **Space-borne platforms** → Satellites (e.g., INSAT, NOAA, Sentinel-1 SAR) - **Air-borne platforms** → Aircraft, Drones (UAVs), Helicopters - **Ground-based platforms** → Seismographs, river gauges, weather stations, IoT sensors - **Crowd-sourced platforms** → Mobile apps, social media feeds **Why it matters:** Without accurate *real-time data*, all downstream decisions are guesswork. --- ### 🔹 Layer 2: Data Processing & Analysis Layer The raw data is processed, geo-tagged, and transformed into usable intelligence: - **GIS** overlays multiple spatial layers (population × flood depth × building type) - **AI/ML models** classify damage from satellite imagery - **Hydrological models** predict flood hydrographs - **Seismic models** estimate earthquake magnitude and aftershock probability --- ### 🔹 Layer 3: Communication & Dissemination Layer The processed intelligence is pushed to last-mile users: - **Cellular Broadcast (CBS)** → SMS to all phones in a geographic cell - **Satellite-based sirens** → INMARSAT, BGAN terminals - **Social media push** → Twitter/X, WhatsApp Business API - **TV/Radio CAP protocol** → Common Alerting Protocol (ITU-T X.1303) --- ### 🔹 Layer 4: Decision Support & Action Layer - **Emergency Operations Centres (EOCs)** visualize dashboards - **Resource allocation algorithms** dispatch nearest ambulances - **Evacuation routing** uses real-time road network graph algorithms --- ## 2.2 Technology-MPRR Phase Cross-Mapping Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Favourite Table — Memorize this for 14-mark questions:** | Technology | Mitigation | Preparedness | Response | Recovery | |---|---|---|---|---| | **GIS** | Hazard zonation, vulnerability mapping | Resource pre-positioning maps | Damage assessment maps | Reconstruction planning | | **GPS** | Asset inventory geotagging | Vehicle fleet tracking | Rescue team navigation | Insurance claim mapping | | **Remote Sensing** | Land-use change detection | Base map preparation | Flood extent mapping | Post-disaster building inventory | | **EWS** | Risk modelling | Drills & mock alerts | Sirens, SMS alerts | Lessons-learned updates | | **Drones** | Slope stability surveys | Route reconnaissance | Live aerial footage | Damage assessment | | **AI/ML** | Predictive risk models | Scenario simulations | Debris detection | Recovery progress tracking | | **Social Media** | Risk communication | Community engagement | Situational reports from citizens | Survivor tracing | --- ## 2.3 Core Operational Frameworks ### 🔸 A. GIS — The "Decision Lens" A GIS integrates **five components**: 1. **Hardware** (workstations, GPS receivers) 2. **Software** (ArcGIS, QGIS, Google Earth Engine) 3. **Data** (spatial + attribute) 4. **People** (GIS analysts) 5. **Methods/Procedures** (workflows) **Vector vs Raster Data Model:** $$ \text{Vector Model: } \text{Point} = (x, y) \quad \text{Line} = \{(x_1,y_1), (x_2,y_2), \dots\} \quad \text{Polygon} = \text{Closed polyline} $$ $$ \text{Raster Model: } A_{i,j} = f(\text{pixel at row } i, \text{ column } j) $$ --- ### 🔸 B. Remote Sensing — The "All-Seeing Eye" **Fundamental Equation of Electromagnetic Remote Sensing:** $$ L_{\text{sensed}} = \frac{E_{\text{sun}} \cdot \rho \cdot \cos\theta}{\pi \cdot d^2} \cdot \tau_{\text{atm}} + L_{\text{path}} $$ Where: - $L_{\text{sensed}}$ = Radiance reaching sensor - $E_{\text{sun}}$ = Solar irradiance - $\rho$ = Surface reflectance (target property) - $\theta$ = Sun zenith angle - $d$ = Sun-Earth distance - $\tau_{\text{atm}}$ = Atmospheric transmittance - $L_{\text{path}}$ = Path radiance (scattered) **Why it matters:** By measuring $L_{\text{sensed}}$ and knowing all other terms, we **solve for $\rho$**, which reveals *what the surface is made of* (water, vegetation, concrete, fire). --- ### 🔸 C. GPS Trilateration A GPS receiver computes its position $(x, y, z)$ by measuring distance to at least **4 satellites**: $$ d_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} = c \cdot (t_i - t_0) $$ Where: - $d_i$ = Distance to satellite $i$ - $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ = Known satellite position - $c$ = Speed of light $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - $t_i$ = Signal travel time from satellite $i$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 4 satellites and not 3?** The 4th equation resolves the **clock bias** of the receiver, since cheap civilian clocks are not perfectly synchronized with atomic clocks on satellites. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **📌 EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Key Equations to Memorize** | # | Formula | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ | GPS range from time delay | meters | | 2 | $GSD = \frac{H \cdot p}{f}$ | Ground Sampling Distance (drone image resolution) | m/pixel | | 3 | $R = \frac{c}{2} \cdot \tau$ | Radar range resolution | meters | | 4 | $NDVI = \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | Normalized Difference Vegetation Index | dimensionless [-1, +1] | | 5 | $NDBI = \frac{SWIR - NIR}{SWIR + NIR}$ | Normalized Difference Built-up Index | dimensionless | | 6 | $S = k \cdot \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ | SAR backscatter (dB) | decibels | | 7 | $T_{\text{lead}} = T_{\text{event}} - T_{\text{alert}}$ | EWS lead time | hours | | 8 | $P_{\text{false}} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False alarm rate | ratio | | 9 | $A_{\text{accuracy}} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | Overall AI classification accuracy | ratio | | 10 | $v = \frac{d}{t}$ | Vehicle routing speed for evac logistics | km/hr | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symbol Substitution Rule:** In KTU exam answer sheets, replace $\rho$ with `rho`, $\theta$ with `theta`, $\tau$ with `tau` if LaTeX is not allowed. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Technology | Real-World Application | KTU-Style Insight | |---|---|---| | GIS + RS | Kerala State Disaster Management Authority's flood inundation atlas | Used in 2018, 2019, 2024 Kerala floods | | GPS | Tracking NDRF teams during Wayanad landslide (2024) | Real-time coordination | | Drones | NDRF's UAV deployment in Joshimath subsidence (2023) | Thermal imaging for survivors | | AI/ML | Google's Flood Hub — ML-based flood forecasting in India | Operational in Bihar, Assam | | EWS | India Meteorological Department's Cyclone Warning Dissemination System | 10-minute cyclone updates | # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: GPS Position Fix via Linearization **Problem:** Given pseudoranges $\rho_i$ to 4 satellites at known positions $(X_i, Y_i, Z_i)$, find receiver coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and clock bias $b$. **Step 1 — Write the nonlinear pseudorange equation:** $$ \rho_i = \sqrt{(x - X_i)^2 + (y - Y_i)^2 + (z - Z_i)^2} + c \cdot b $$ **Step 2 — Linearize using Taylor series around an approximate position $(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0)$:** Let $\Delta x = x - x_0$, $\Delta y = y - y_0$, $\Delta z = z - z_0$, $\Delta b = b - b_0$. The range from approximate position to satellite $i$ is: $$ r_i^0 = \sqrt{(x_0 - X_i)^2 + (y_0 - Y_i)^2 + (z_0 - Z_i)^2} $$ **Step 3 — Take partial derivatives (the geometry of trilateration):** $$ \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x} = \frac{-(x_0 - X_i)}{r_i^0} = -l_i $$ $$ \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y} = \frac{-(y_0 - Y_i)}{r_i^0} = -m_i $$ $$ \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z} = \frac{-(z_0 - Z_i)}{r_i^0} = -n_i $$ $$ \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial b} = c $$ **Step 4 — Form the linear system (4 equations, 4 unknowns):** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta\rho_1 &= -l_1 \, \Delta x - m_1 \, \Delta y - n_1 \, \Delta z + c \, \Delta b \\ \Delta\rho_2 &= -l_2 \, \Delta x - m_2 \, \Delta y - n_2 \, \Delta z + c \, \Delta b \\ \Delta\rho_3 &= -l_3 \, \Delta x - m_3 \, \Delta y - n_3 \, \Delta z + c \, \Delta b \\ \Delta\rho_4 &= -l_4 \, \Delta x - m_4 \, \Delta y - n_4 \, \Delta z + c \, \Delta b \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Solve iteratively using least-squares (Gauss-Newton method):** $$ \Delta \mathbf{X} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta\boldsymbol{\rho} $$ **Step 6 — Update position estimate:** $$ x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta x, \quad y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta y, \quad z_{k+1} = z_k + \Delta z $$ Iteration continues until $|\Delta x|, |\Delta y|, |\Delta z| < 10^{-3}$ m (convergence threshold). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** This is why GPS gives position only after a few seconds — it needs iterative convergence. *Receivers display "cold start" delay because of this linearization process.* --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: NDVI as a Flood Detection Index **Problem:** Prove that NDVI drops sharply over water bodies post-flood, allowing automated flood mapping. **Step 1 — Define spectral reflectances:** - $R_{\text{RED}}$ = Reflectance in Red band (0.62–0.69 μm) - $R_{\text{NIR}}$ = Reflectance in NIR band (0.76–0.90 μm) **Step 2 — Recall spectral signatures:** | Surface | $R_{\text{RED}}$ | $R_{\text{NIR}}$ | $NDVI$ | |---|---|---|---| | Dense Vegetation | Low (0.05) | High (0.50) | ≈ +0.82 | | Bare Soil | Medium (0.20) | Medium (0.30) | ≈ +0.20 | | Water (Clear) | Medium (0.10) | Very Low (0.02) | ≈ -0.66 | | Water (Turbid Flood) | Higher (0.20) | Low (0.10) | ≈ -0.33 | **Step 3 — Apply the formula:** $$ NDVI = \frac{R_{\text{NIR}} - R_{\text{RED}}}{R_{\text{NIR}} + R_{\text{RED}}} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical verification for a flood pixel:** Suppose $R_{\text{RED}} = 0.18$, $R_{\text{NIR}} = 0.08$ (typical turbid flood water). $$ \begin{aligned} NDVI &= \frac{0.08 - 0.18}{0.08 + 0.18} \\ &= \frac{-0.10}{0.26} \\ &\approx -0.385 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Threshold the image:** Any pixel with $NDVI < -0.10$ is classified as **flood water**. This is the standard ISRO-NRSC flood mapping protocol used during Kerala 2018 floods. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: EWS Lead Time and False Alarm Trade-off **Problem:** Relate lead time to false alarm rate using basic signal detection theory. **Step 1 — Define the four outcomes:** | Reality \ Prediction | Alarm Issued | No Alarm | |---|---|---| | Event Occurs | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) | | Event Does Not Occur | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) | **Step 2 — Define the False Alarm Rate (FAR):** $$ FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} $$ **Step 3 — Define the Probability of Detection (POD):** $$ POD = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$ **Step 4 — The fundamental EWS trade-off:** Longer lead time → more uncertainty → higher FAR. Shorter lead time → less time to act → higher FN (missed events). $$ \text{Optimal Threshold} \implies \min \left[ C_{\text{false alarm}} \cdot FAR + C_{\text{miss}} \cdot (1 - POD) \right] $$ Where $C_{\text{false alarm}}$ = economic + psychological cost of false alarm, and $C_{\text{miss}}$ = cost of missed disaster. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Real-world example:** IMD's cyclone forecasts have improved lead time from 24 hours (2005) to 72 hours (2020), but FAR in Bay of Bengal cyclones still hovers around 25–30%. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Technology Selection During Disasters | Disaster Type | Primary Tech | Secondary Tech | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | Earthquake | Seismic sensors + GIS | Drones (post-event) | Cannot predict precise time | | Flood | SAR satellites + NDVI | SMS-based EWS | Cloud cover (optical sensors) | | Cyclone | Doppler radar + EWS | Social media monitoring | Coastal population evacuation | | Landslide | IoT slope sensors + GPS | Drone photogrammetry | Sensor maintenance in remote areas | | Wildfire | Thermal IR satellites | UAVs with thermal cams | Smoke obscures optical view | | Tsunami | DART buoys + sea-level sensors | SMS + sirens | < 1 hour lead time typical | | Pandemic | AI/ML mobility models | GIS + dashboards | Data privacy concerns | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Disaster Management Technology Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB DATA1[Satellite Sensors

Define GIS and explain its role in disaster management.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** A **Geographic Information System (GIS)** is a computer-based system designed to capture, store, manipulate, analyse, manage, and present spatial or geographic data. It integrates **hardware, software, data, people, and procedures** to support decision-making. **Role in Disaster Management (1.5 marks):** GIS plays a critical role across all four phases of the disaster management cycle: 1. **Mitigation:** Hazard zonation, vulnerability mapping, landslide susceptibility. 2. **Preparedness:** Resource pre-positioning, evacuation route planning. 3. **Response:** Real-time damage assessment, tracking of rescue teams using GPS. 4. **Recovery:** Reconstruction planning, insurance claim verification. *Example:* ISRO's **Bhuvan** platform provides GIS-based flood inundation maps used by the Kerala State Disaster Management Authority. --- ### **Q2. What is an Early Warning System (EWS)? List its four key components.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** An **Early Warning System (EWS)** is an integrated chain of activities that delivers timely, effective, and actionable information to enable individuals, communities, and organizations to prepare and act appropriately to reduce the risk of harm before a hazardous event. **Four Key Components (2 marks):** 1. **Hazard Monitoring & Detection** — Sensors, satellites, radars. 2. **Risk Assessment & Modelling** — Forecasting, scenario simulation. 3. **Communication & Dissemination** — CAP, SMS, sirens, broadcast media. 4. **Response Capability** — Community preparedness, evacuation plans. --- --- ## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Format) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Option Set 1* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Adapted]` > **"With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an Early Warning System (EWS). Discuss in detail the role of GIS, GPS, and Remote Sensing in disaster preparedness and response phases. Mention two real-world Indian case studies."** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: EWS Architecture & Components** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (7 marks):** An Early Warning System is a **four-stage chain** that converts hazard data into community action. **Stage 1 — Hazard Monitoring (1.5 marks):** Continuous observation using seismographs, Doppler weather radar, tide gauges, river water-level sensors, and satellites (e.g., INSAT-3D for cyclone tracking). **Stage 2 — Modelling & Forecasting (1.5 marks):** Numerical weather prediction models, hydrodynamic flood models (e.g., MIKE 11), and seismic magnitude models process raw data into forecasts with probability estimates. **Stage 3 — Risk Assessment (1 mark):** Combining hazard probability with **vulnerability** and **exposure** data via GIS to estimate *who* and *what* is at risk. **Stage 4 — Communication & Response (1.5 marks):** Issuing alerts through the **Common Alerting Protocol (CAP)** via SMS, sirens, TV, radio, and social media. IMD uses this chain during cyclones. **Block diagram (1.5 marks):** *[Draw the 4-stage chain as in Section 4.3]* --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: GIS + GPS + Remote Sensing Roles + Case Studies** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (7 marks):** **GIS (2 marks):** - Stores spatial layers (population, infrastructure, hazard zones). - Performs overlay analysis to identify high-risk areas. - Generates evacuation route maps using **Network Analyst**. **GPS (1.5 marks):** - Tracks NDRF rescue vehicles in real time. - Geotags victims during search operations. - Improves positional accuracy to **±3 m** using WAAS-enabled receivers. **Remote Sensing (1.5 marks):** - Optical satellites (Landsat-8, Sentinel-2) provide **multispectral imagery** for damage mapping. - SAR satellites (Sentinel-1) penetrate clouds and are used for **all-weather flood mapping**. **Indian Case Studies (2 marks):** 1. **Kerala Floods 2018:** ISRO used Sentinel-1 SAR data to map flood inundation across 5.4 million hectares, helping authorities identify 341 relief camps. 2. **Cyclone Fani 2019:** IMD's EWS with 72-hour lead time enabled evacuation of 1.2 million people in Odisha, reducing casualties to **64 deaths** (vs. 10,000 in 1999 super-cyclone). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Adapted]` > **"Explain the role of Remote Sensing in disaster management. With suitable diagrams, describe (i) the principle of NDVI-based flood mapping, and (ii) the operational use of UAVs (drones) in post-disaster damage assessment."** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Remote Sensing & NDVI Flood Mapping** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Remote sensing is the acquisition of information about an object/phenomenon using sensors mounted on **spaceborne, airborne, or ground-based platforms** without physical contact. **Types (2 marks):** - **Passive sensors** detect natural radiation (sun-reflected): Landsat, Sentinel-2. - **Active sensors** emit their own signal: Radar (SAR), LiDAR. **NDVI Principle (3 marks):** $$ NDVI = \frac{NIR - RED}{NIR + RED} $$ - **Water:** Low NIR, moderate RED → **NDVI < 0** - **Vegetation:** High NIR, low RED → **NDVI > 0.5** - **Turbid flood water:** Slightly higher RED, low NIR → **NDVI ≈ -0.3 to -0.1** **Procedure:** Pre-flood and post-flood NDVI images are differenced. Pixels showing **NDVI drop > 0.2** are classified as flood-inundated. **Example (1 mark):** During Kerala 2018 floods, this method identified **>5.4 million hectares** of inundated area within 48 hours of the event. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: UAVs in Damage Assessment** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (7 marks):** **What is a UAV? (1 mark):** An Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) is a remotely piloted aircraft used for aerial imaging, surveillance, and payload delivery in disaster zones. **Types (1 mark):** - **Multi-rotor** (Quadcopter) → Vertical take-off, used for close-range mapping. - **Fixed-wing** → Long endurance, used for area-wide surveys. **Operational Workflow (3 marks):** 1. **Flight planning** — Define grid pattern with 80% frontal and 60% side overlap. 2. **Image acquisition** — Capture hundreds to thousands of overlapping images. 3. **Photogrammetry (SfM)** — Software like Pix4D reconstructs 3D point cloud. 4. **Orthomosaic & DEM** — Generate georeferenced maps. 5. **AI classification** — Deep learning models (e.g., CNNs) classify damaged vs. intact buildings. 6. **GIS integration** — Output added as layer to municipal GIS. **Advantages (1 mark):** - Rapid deployment (<1 hour). - High resolution (**GSD 1–5 cm**). - Operates in inaccessible terrain. **Limitations (1 mark):** - Limited flight time (**25–40 min**). - Restricted by weather (wind, rain). - Regulatory airspace restrictions in India (DGCA guidelines). **Example:** NDRF deployed UAVs in **Joshimath subsidence (2023)** to map ground cracks and building damage. --- --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **🚨 Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks — Module 4** > > 1. **Confusing "Active" vs "Passive" Sensors** (–2 marks): Many students write "RADAR is passive." It is **ACTIVE** — it emits its own microwave pulse. > > 2. **Forgetting the "4th Satellite" in GPS** (–1 mark): Always state *why* 4 satellites are needed: the 4th equation resolves **receiver clock bias**. Examiners specifically look for this. > > 3. **Writing "RS" without mentioning Wavelength Bands** (–1 mark): Always specify the band (e.g., "Sentinel-1 C-band SAR at 5.6 cm wavelength"). > > 4. **GIS described as "software only"** (–2 marks): GIS is a **system** with 5 components (Hardware, Software, Data, People, Methods). Writing only "ArcGIS is GIS" loses half the marks. > > 5. **EWS = "Siren" only** (–2 marks): EWS is a **4-stage chain** (Monitoring → Forecasting → Decision → Communication → Response). Sirens are only the last-mile tool. > > 6. **No Indian case study** (–1 to –2 marks): KTU examiners reward **"Made in India"** examples: Bhuvan, IMD cyclone warnings, ISRO disaster management support, NDRF drone use. > > 7. **Mixing up NDVI and NDBI** (–1 mark): NDVI = vegetation, NDBI = built-up. Don't get them swapped in the formula. > > 8. **Skipping block diagram in EWS** (–1 mark): A 14-mark EWS question without a diagram loses 1–2 marks minimum. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 4** > > ✅ **GIS** = 5 components (HW, SW, Data, People, Methods); vector vs raster data. > > ✅ **GPS** = Trilateration from ≥4 satellites; equation $d = c \cdot \Delta t$; 4th satellite removes clock bias. > > ✅ **Remote Sensing** = Passive (sun-dependent) vs Active (self-emitting); optical vs SAR (cloud-penetrating). > > ✅ **NDVI** formula = $(NIR - RED)/(NIR + RED)$; values -1 (water) to +1 (dense vegetation). > > ✅ **NDBI** = $(SWIR - NIR)/(SWIR + NIR)$; detects built-up areas for urban flood/heat studies. > > ✅ **EWS** = 4-stage chain: Monitoring → Forecasting → Decision → Communication → Response. > > ✅ **Lead Time** = Event time − Alert time; optimal trade-off with **False Alarm Rate**. > > ✅ **UAV/Drones** = GSD = $(H \cdot p)/f$; 25–40 min flight time; 80%/60% image overlap standard. > > ✅ **AI/ML** = Accuracy = $(TP + TN)/Total$; uses CNNs for satellite image damage classification. > > ✅ **Crowdsourcing** = Public-reported data via mobile apps (e.g., **Ushahidi**); used heavily in 2015 Nepal earthquake. > > ✅ **CAP Protocol** = Common Alerting Protocol (ITU-T X.1303); global standard for multi-channel emergency alerts. > > ✅ **Sendai Framework** = 4 Priorities; technology enables Priorities 1, 3, and 4. > > ✅ **Indian Platforms to remember:** Bhuvan (ISRO), IMD cyclone EWS, NDRF drones, Google Flood Hub, Sentinel Asia. > > ✅ **Real Exam Mantra:** Always end a 14-mark answer with **2 Indian case studies** + **1 international reference** + **a neat labelled block diagram**.
